/* キリコ祭りを紹介する施設 */
revision.text:
{{Redirect|キリコ祭り|小惑星|キリコ祭り (小惑星)}} {{参照方法|date=2022年12月}} [[File:Noto Yosakoi Wakura 01.jpg|thumb|right|300px|七尾市で行われる石崎奉燈祭]]
'''能登キリコ祭り'''（のとキリコまつり）は、[[石川県]]の[[能登半島]]各地で行われる[[祭|祭り]]である<ref name="bluesignal">{{Cite journal|和書|url = https://www.westjr.co.jp/company/info/issue/bsignal/19_vol_185/issue/01.html|title = 日本遺産を訪ねる西への旅 神をいざなう能登の祭り|journal = Blue Signal|publisher = [[西日本旅客鉄道]]|date = 2019-07|issue = 2019年7月号|accessdate = 2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="sankeiphoto">{{Cite web|和書|url = https://www.sankei.com/photo/story/news/191006/sty1910060001-n1.html|title = 【美しきにっぽん】夢幻 漆黒照らす燈籠 能登半島・キリコ祭り|website = 産経フォト|date = 2019-10-02|accessdate = 2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="asa20220529">{{Cite news|url = https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|title = 能登の祭りの代表格「キリコ」 土屋太鳳さん、自衛隊も担ぐ|newspaper = [[朝日新聞デジタル]]|archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20220529163550/https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|date = 2022-05-29|archivedate = 2022-05-29|accessdate = 2022-12-30}}</ref>。
== 概要 == 切籠（キリコ）または奉燈（ほうとう）と呼ばれる切子[[灯籠]]を使うことを特徴とするものである<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}。[[高さ]]は10[[メートル]]を超える巨大なキリコもあるが、4メートルから6メートルのものが主流で20人前後が担ぎ手としてキリコを担ぐ<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}}。地域によって御明かし（おあかし）とも呼ばれる。市街地を練り歩き、[[御旅所]]（多くは水辺や広場）に[[渡御]]し[[神社]]に戻るのが一般的で{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}、祭りによっては[[海面|海上]]を渡御するものもある{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}。
主に[[7月]]から[[9月]]に掛けて夏祭り・秋祭りとして{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134-135}}、能登半島の約200か所で行われており<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="sankeiphoto"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}、[[疫病]]退散<ref name="bluesignal"/>または[[大漁]]・[[豊作]]<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>を願って始まったとされるものが多い。
== 歴史 == <!-- 『まれ』についての具体的な記述は、検証不可能な記述として一部削除していますのでご了承願います。また、マークアップした部分についての出典提示が今後もない場合は、記述を削除します --> キリコ祭りの歴史は伝承の中にあるものがあり、文献による記録はそう多くないが、[[1647年]]（[[正保]]3年）の輪島住吉神社の祭礼定書にキリコのことが書かれている<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>。{{要出典範囲|宇出津・八坂神社のキリコ祭り（あばれ祭り）については[[寛文]]年間（1661年-1673年）に桜井源五という人が始めたと伝承されているが、これについては異論もある|date=2022年12月}}。祭りは[[江戸時代]]の発祥が起源とされ{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}、由来は祭りによって異なるが[[京都]]の[[祇園祭]]に由来または夏超しの[[神事]]に由来するものに分けられる<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}。
[[1997年]]（[[平成]]9年）[[12月4日]]、「能登のキリコ祭り」として国の「[[記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財]]」に選択された<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|161401|能登のキリコ祭り}}</ref>。
[[2015年]]（平成27年）に放送されたNHK[[連続テレビ小説]]『[[まれ]]』では、ヒロイン役の[[土屋太鳳]]が劇中でキリコを担いで祭りに参加している<ref name="asa20220529"/>。また、同年[[4月24日]]には、「灯り舞う半島 能登 ～熱狂のキリコ祭り～」の名称で[[文化庁]]による[[日本遺産]]に認定された<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref>[http://www.bunka.go.jp/seisaku/bunkazai/nihon_isan/ichiran.html これまでに認定された「日本遺産」一覧] 文化庁、2018年12月5日</ref>。
== キリコ == [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (13).jpg|thumb|並べられたキリコ（輪島キリコ会館）]] [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (6).jpg|thumb|キリコ吉祥文字]]
キリコ（切籠）は、代表的な形としては背が高い[[直方体]]状の[[行灯]]で<ref name="bluesignal"/>、前面中央部には[[漢字]]3文字で表した「キリコ吉祥文字」と呼ばれる地区ごとの願いや祈りを込めた文字を配し<ref name="bluesignal"/>、「後美人」と呼ばれる背面には様々な絵（[[武者絵]]や[[風景画]]など）が施されている<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}}。上部は[[切妻屋根]]の構造で{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}}、[[提灯]]や[[御幣]]などの飾りが付けられている{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}}。ただ実際のキリコの形や大きさはその地区・地区により様々である{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}}。中には[[青森ねぶた]]にも似た曲線状のものも存在する。
キリコは「笹キリコ」と呼ばれる[[ササ|笹]][[竹]]の先端に[[和紙]]を付けた手で持てる程度のものが始まりとされており<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>、次第に巨大化していき、江戸時代後期には現在のキリコになったとされている<ref name="asa20220529"/>。また、近年は[[過疎|過疎化]]に伴い担ぎ手が不足している地域があり、[[宝立七夕キリコ祭り]]では日程を[[2019年]]（[[令和]]元年）からこれまでの[[8月7日]]の開催日を8月の第1土曜日に変更した例もある<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>。
== 主なキリコ祭り == 記載している期日は年度によって変動がある。 ; [[七尾市]] * 互市祭 - 7月上旬 * [[七尾祇園祭]] - 7月上旬 * [[能登島]][[向田の火祭り]] - 7月最終土曜日 *[[六保祭|六保納涼祭]] - 7月最終土曜日 * なごしの祭り - 7月下旬 * 塩津かがり火恋まつり - 7月第4土曜日 * [[石崎奉燈祭]]<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}} - 8月第1土曜日{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}} * [[新宮納涼祭]] - [[8月14日]]
; [[輪島市]] * 剱地八幡神社大祭（おさよ祭り） - 7月中旬 * 水無月祭り - 7月下旬 * 名舟大祭 - 7月下旬 * 曽々木大祭 - 8月17日 * 輪島大祭（奥津姫神社大祭・重蔵神社大祭<ref name="asa20220529"/>：住吉神社大祭・輪島前神社大祭の総称）<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}} - [[8月22日]]{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}} - [[8月25日]]{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}}
; [[珠洲市]] * [[飯田町燈籠山祭り]]<ref name="bluesignal"/> - 7月下旬 * [[宝立七夕キリコ祭り]]<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="sankeiphoto"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}} - 8月第1土曜日<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>（[[2018年]]までは[[8月7日]]{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}}） * 蛸島キリコ祭り - 9月10日、11日 * 正院キリコ祭り - 9月中旬 * 寺家キリコ祭り - 9月第2土日 * 馬緤キリコ祭り - 10月13、14日
; [[鳳珠郡]][[穴水町]] * 中居キリコ祭り - 7月下旬 * 明千寺キリコ祭り - 8月中旬 * 沖波大漁祭り<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}} - [[8月14日]]・[[8月15日]]{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}} * 大町・川島祭り - 9月中旬 * 前波曳山祭 - 9月中旬
; 鳳珠郡[[能登町]] * [[あばれ祭り|あばれ祭]]<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}}<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url = https://www.ntv.co.jp/showchannel/articles/21271o3nq9agacxvp59j.html|title = 松下洸平が櫻井翔と名店のカルボナーラに挑戦!風間俊介は能登のあばれ祭へ!【1億3000万人のSHOWチャンネル】|website = [[1億3000万人のSHOWチャンネル]]|date = 2022-07-30|accessdate = 2022-12-30}}</ref> - 7月第1金・土曜日{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}} * 藤波キリコ祭り - 7月14日・15日 * 姫どいやさ祭 - 7月第4土・日曜日 * 松波人形キリコ祭り - 7月第4土曜日 * 恋路火祭り - 7月27日 * ござれ祭り - 8月中旬 * にわか祭 - 8月24日 * 波並大祭 - 9月第1金・土曜日 * 柳田大祭 - 9月16日 * 小木袖キリコ祭り - 9月第3土・日曜日
; [[羽咋郡]][[志賀町]] * 大福寺祭 - 8月上旬 * 西海祭り<ref name="bluesignal"/> - 8月14日 * 酒見祭 - 8月下旬 * 富来八朔祭り - 8月下旬 * 福浦祭 - 8月下旬 * 笹波・前浜祭 - 8月下旬
== キリコ祭りを紹介する施設 == [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (3).jpg|thumb|輪島キリコ会館]]
=== 輪島キリコ会館 === {{Main|輪島キリコ会館}}
石川県[[輪島市]]マリンタウンには[[輪島キリコ会館]]がある<ref name="asa20220529"/>。大小のキリコ31基を常設展示し、キリコ祭りを紹介している<ref name="asa20220529"/>。1979年（昭和54年）6月、塚田町3-22-2に前身の能登のお祭り館 キリコ会館が開館した。2015年（平成27年）3月29日、マリンタウンに移転して輪島キリコ会館としてリニューアルオープンした<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://wajima-kiriko.com/news/20150328/ |title=いよいよ明日輪島キリコ会館オープンです！|publisher=輪島キリコ会館|date=2015-03-28|accessdate=2020-08-27}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.notonosho.co.jp/blog/2015/03/29/%E3%82%AD%E3%83%AA%E3%82%B3%E4%BC%9A%E9%A4%A8%E6%9C%AC%E6%97%A5%E3%83%AA%E3%83%8B%E3%83%A5%E3%83%BC%E3%82%A2%E3%83%AB%E3%82%AA%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97%E3%83%B3%EF%BC%81%E7%9F%B3%E5%B7%9D%E7%9C%8C%E3%80%80/ |title=キリコ会館 本日リニューアルオープン|publisher= 輪島ねぶた温泉能登の庄|date=2015-03-29|accessdate=2020-08-27}}</ref>。
=== 和倉温泉お祭り会館 === {{Main|和倉温泉お祭り会館}}
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == {{参照方法|date=2022年12月|section=1}} <!-- リーフレットは検証できる文献とはなりません。脚注引用していない文献は、削除される場合がありますのでご注意願います --> * 北國総合研究所（編）『日本遺産 能登のキリコ祭り』[[北國新聞社]]、2016年 ISBN 978-4-8330-2068-8 * {{Cite book|和書|title = ニッポンを解剖する!金沢 能登図鑑|publisher = [[JTBパブリッシング]]|date = 2018-03-01|isbn = 978-4-533-12465-5|ref = {{sfnref|JTB|2018}} }}
== 関連項目 == * [[キリコ祭り (小惑星)]] * [[和倉温泉お祭り会館]]
== 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Noto Kiriko Matsuri}} * [https://www.hot-ishikawa.jp/feature/kirikomatsuri/top ほっと石川旅ねっと 巨大な灯籠が乱舞 魂をゆさぶる 能登の「キリコ祭り」特集!!] - 石川県観光連盟 * [https://japan-heritage.bunka.go.jp/ja/stories/story004/ 灯（あか）り舞う半島 能登] - 日本遺産ポータルサイト（文化庁） * [http://www.notokiriko.jp/index.html 能登キリコ祭り] - 能登キリコ広報委員会（能登キリコネットワーク)

revision.text:
{{Redirect|Noto Kiriko Matsuri|asteroid|Kiriko Matsuri (asteroid)}} {{Referencing style|date=December 2022}} [[File:Noto Yosakoi Wakura 01.jpg|thumb|right|300px|The {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭}} held in {{ill|Nanao|ja|七尾市}}.]]
'''Noto Kiriko Matsuri''' (能登キリコ祭り) are festivals held across the {{ill|Noto Peninsula|ja|能登半島}} in {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|ja|石川県}}.<ref name="bluesignal">{{Cite journal|title=Travel West to Japan Heritage: Noto's Festivals that Invite the Gods|journal=Blue Signal|publisher={{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}}|date=July 2019|issue=July 2019|url=https://www.westjr.co.jp/company/info/issue/bsignal/19_vol_185/issue/01.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="sankeiphoto">{{Cite web|title=The Sublime Beauty of Japan: Lantern Festivals on the Noto Peninsula|website=Sankei Photo|date=October 2, 2019|url=https://www.sankei.com/photo/story/news/191006/sty1910060001-n1.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="asa20220529">{{Cite news|title=Noto's Iconic Kiriko Festivals: Taō Tsuchiya and the Self-Defense Forces Join In|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun Digital|date=May 29, 2022|url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20220529163550/https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archivedate=May 29, 2022|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref>.
== Overview == The festivals are characterized by the use of tall, rectangular lantern floats called {{ill|Kiriko|ja|切籠}} or {{ill|Hōtō|ja|奉燈}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} While some kiriko lanterns exceed 10 meters in height, most are between 4 to 6 meters and are carried by groups of around 20 people.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} In some areas, they are also referred to as "Oakashii" (御明かし). The festivals typically feature processions through town streets to a temporary shrine (often located near water or in open spaces) before returning to the main shrine.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Some festivals include sea crossings during their processions.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
Held primarily from July to September as summer or autumn festivals, they occur at about 200 locations throughout the peninsula.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="sankeiphoto"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Many of these festivals originated with prayers for the prevention of epidemics<ref name="bluesignal"/> or for bountiful harvests and fishing.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== History == The origins of the Kiriko Matsuri are partly rooted in oral traditions, with limited documentation. One of the earliest references to kiriko can be found in the 1647 (Shōhō 3) festival records of {{ill|Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|輪島住吉神社}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> The {{ill|Abare Matsuri|ja|あばれ祭}} of {{ill|Ushitsu|ja|宇出津}}, held at {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社 (能登町)}}, is said to have started during the Kanbun era (1661–1673), although this is debated.<ref name="asa20220529"/> The festivals are generally believed to have originated during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} and derive either from the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}} in Kyoto or from midsummer Shinto rites.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
In December 1997 (Heisei 9), the "Noto Kiriko Matsuri" was designated as a {{ill|Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財}} by the national government.<ref>{{Cultural Heritage Online|161401|Noto's Kiriko Festivals}}</ref> In 2015, the festivals were recognized as part of the Japan Heritage program under the title "Islands of Light: Noto Peninsula's Passionate Kiriko Festivals."<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref>[http://www.bunka.go.jp/seisaku/bunkazai/nihon_isan/ichiran.html List of Japan Heritage Sites] - Agency for Cultural Affairs</ref>
== Kiriko == [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (13).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lanterns on display at the Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]] [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (6).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lantern with auspicious characters]]
Kiriko lanterns are typically tall, rectangular {{ill|Andon|ja|行灯}} with "auspicious characters" (吉祥文字) representing prayers and wishes inscribed on the front.<ref name="bluesignal"/> The back often features elaborate paintings, such as warrior imagery or landscapes.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} The lanterns are topped with a {{ill|Kirizuma roof|ja|切妻屋根}} and adorned with decorations like paper streamers and {{ill|Chōchin|ja|提灯}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} Designs and dimensions vary by region, with some kiriko resembling the curved shapes of {{ill|Nebuta|ja|青森ねぶた}} floats.
It is believed that kiriko originated as small, hand-held bamboo sticks with paper lanterns ("Sasa Kiriko") and gradually grew larger, reaching their current size by the late Edo period.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> However, depopulation has led to a shortage of carriers in some regions, prompting adjustments like rescheduling festivals to weekends.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Major Kiriko Festivals == Dates vary annually. Listed below are notable examples:
; Nanao * {{ill|Mutomachi Festival|ja|互市祭}} - Early July * {{ill|Nanao Gion Matsuri|ja|七尾祇園祭}} - Early July * {{ill|Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|ja|向田の火祭り}} - Last Saturday of July * {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}}
; Wajima * Wajima Taisai - August 22–25<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}}
; Suzu * {{ill|Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|ja|飯田町燈籠山祭り}} - Late July * {{ill|Hōraku Matsuri|ja|宝立七夕キリコ祭り}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Kiriko Festival Facilities == === Wajima Kiriko Art Museum === {{Main|Wajima Kiriko Art Museum}} The museum in {{ill|Wajima|ja|輪島市}} showcases 31 kiriko lanterns and explains the festivals.<ref name="asa20220529"/> Opened in 2015, it serves as a cultural hub for the events.<ref>[https://wajima-kiriko.com/ Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
{{Redirect|Noto Kiriko Matsuri|asteroid|Kiriko Matsuri (asteroid)}} {{Referencing style|date=December 2022}} [[File:Noto Yosakoi Wakura 01.jpg|thumb|right|300px|The {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|12=draft}} held in {{ill|Nanao|ja|七尾市|12=draft}}.]]
'''Noto Kiriko Matsuri''' (能登キリコ祭り) are festivals held across the {{ill|Noto Peninsula|ja|能登半島|12=draft}} in {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|ja|石川県|12=draft}}.<ref name="bluesignal">{{Cite journal|title=Travel West to Japan Heritage: Noto's Festivals that Invite the Gods|journal=Blue Signal|publisher={{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道|12=draft}}|date=July 2019|issue=July 2019|url=https://www.westjr.co.jp/company/info/issue/bsignal/19_vol_185/issue/01.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="sankeiphoto">{{Cite web|title=The Sublime Beauty of Japan: Lantern Festivals on the Noto Peninsula|website=Sankei Photo|date=October 2, 2019|url=https://www.sankei.com/photo/story/news/191006/sty1910060001-n1.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="asa20220529">{{Cite news|title=Noto's Iconic Kiriko Festivals: Taō Tsuchiya and the Self-Defense Forces Join In|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun Digital|date=May 29, 2022|url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20220529163550/https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archivedate=May 29, 2022|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref>.
== Overview == The festivals are characterized by the use of tall, rectangular lantern floats called {{ill|Kiriko|ja|切籠|12=draft}} or {{ill|Hōtō|ja|奉燈|12=draft}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} While some kiriko lanterns exceed 10 meters in height, most are between 4 to 6 meters and are carried by groups of around 20 people.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} In some areas, they are also referred to as "Oakashii" (御明かし). The festivals typically feature processions through town streets to a temporary shrine (often located near water or in open spaces) before returning to the main shrine.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Some festivals include sea crossings during their processions.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
Held primarily from July to September as summer or autumn festivals, they occur at about 200 locations throughout the peninsula.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="sankeiphoto"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Many of these festivals originated with prayers for the prevention of epidemics<ref name="bluesignal"/> or for bountiful harvests and fishing.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== History == The origins of the Kiriko Matsuri are partly rooted in oral traditions, with limited documentation. One of the earliest references to kiriko can be found in the 1647 (Shōhō 3) festival records of {{ill|Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|輪島住吉神社|12=draft}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> The {{ill|Abare Matsuri|ja|あばれ祭|12=draft}} of {{ill|Ushitsu|ja|宇出津|12=draft}}, held at {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社 (能登町)|12=draft}}, is said to have started during the Kanbun era (1661–1673), although this is debated.<ref name="asa20220529"/> The festivals are generally believed to have originated during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}} and derive either from the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭|12=draft}} in Kyoto or from midsummer Shinto rites.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
In December 1997 (Heisei 9), the "Noto Kiriko Matsuri" was designated as a {{ill|Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財|12=draft}} by the national government.<ref>{{Cultural Heritage Online|161401|Noto's Kiriko Festivals}}</ref> In 2015, the festivals were recognized as part of the Japan Heritage program under the title "Islands of Light: Noto Peninsula's Passionate Kiriko Festivals."<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref>[http://www.bunka.go.jp/seisaku/bunkazai/nihon_isan/ichiran.html List of Japan Heritage Sites] - Agency for Cultural Affairs</ref>
== Kiriko == [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (13).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lanterns on display at the Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]] [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (6).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lantern with auspicious characters]]
Kiriko lanterns are typically tall, rectangular {{ill|Andon|ja|行灯|12=draft}} with "auspicious characters" (吉祥文字) representing prayers and wishes inscribed on the front.<ref name="bluesignal"/> The back often features elaborate paintings, such as warrior imagery or landscapes.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} The lanterns are topped with a {{ill|Kirizuma roof|ja|切妻屋根|12=draft}} and adorned with decorations like paper streamers and {{ill|Chōchin|ja|提灯|12=draft}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} Designs and dimensions vary by region, with some kiriko resembling the curved shapes of {{ill|Nebuta|ja|青森ねぶた|12=draft}} floats.
It is believed that kiriko originated as small, hand-held bamboo sticks with paper lanterns ("Sasa Kiriko") and gradually grew larger, reaching their current size by the late Edo period.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> However, depopulation has led to a shortage of carriers in some regions, prompting adjustments like rescheduling festivals to weekends.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Major Kiriko Festivals == Dates vary annually. Listed below are notable examples:
; Nanao * {{ill|Mutomachi Festival|ja|互市祭|12=draft}} - Early July * {{ill|Nanao Gion Matsuri|ja|七尾祇園祭|12=draft}} - Early July * {{ill|Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|ja|向田の火祭り|12=draft}} - Last Saturday of July * {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|12=draft}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}}
; Wajima * Wajima Taisai - August 22–25<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}}
; Suzu * {{ill|Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|ja|飯田町燈籠山祭り|12=draft}} - Late July * {{ill|Hōraku Matsuri|ja|宝立七夕キリコ祭り|12=draft}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Kiriko Festival Facilities == === Wajima Kiriko Art Museum === {{Main|Wajima Kiriko Art Museum}} The museum in {{ill|Wajima|ja|輪島市|12=draft}} showcases 31 kiriko lanterns and explains the festivals.<ref name="asa20220529"/> Opened in 2015, it serves as a cultural hub for the events.<ref>[https://wajima-kiriko.com/ Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=Nippon Dissected: Kanazawa and Noto Peninsula|publisher=JTB Publishing|date=March 1, 2018|isbn=978-4-533-12465-5|ref={{sfnref|JTB|2018}}}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kiriko Matsuri (asteroid)|ja|キリコ祭り (小惑星)|12=draft}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Noto Kiriko Matsuri}} * [https://www.hot-ishikawa.jp/feature/kirikomatsuri/top Hot Ishikawa Tourism: Kiriko Matsuri] * [https://japan-heritage.bunka.go.jp/ Islands of Light: Noto's Kiriko Festivals] - Japan Heritage

{{draft categories| [[Category:Noto Kiriko Festivals]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Noto Kiriko Matsuri|*]] [[Category:Festivals in Ishikawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Epidemic prevention]] [[Category:Japan Heritage]] [[Category:能登キリコ祭り]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Noto Kiriko Festivals]] [[ja:能登キリコ祭り]]

{{Redirect|Noto Kiriko Matsuri|asteroid|Kiriko Matsuri (asteroid)}} {{Referencing style|date=December 2022}} [[File:Noto Yosakoi Wakura 01.jpg|thumb|right|300px|The {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|12=draft}} held in {{ill|Nanao|ja|七尾市|12=draft}}.]]
'''Noto Kiriko Matsuri''' (能登キリコ祭り) are festivals held across the {{ill|Noto Peninsula|ja|能登半島|12=draft}} in {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|ja|石川県|12=draft}}.<ref name="bluesignal">{{Cite journal|title=Travel West to Japan Heritage: Noto's Festivals that Invite the Gods|journal=Blue Signal|publisher={{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道|12=draft}}|date=July 2019|issue=July 2019|url=https://www.westjr.co.jp/company/info/issue/bsignal/19_vol_185/issue/01.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="sankeiphoto">{{Cite web|title=The Sublime Beauty of Japan: Lantern Festivals on the Noto Peninsula|website=Sankei Photo|date=October 2, 2019|url=https://www.sankei.com/photo/story/news/191006/sty1910060001-n1.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="asa20220529">{{Cite news|title=Noto's Iconic Kiriko Festivals: Taō Tsuchiya and the Self-Defense Forces Join In|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun Digital|date=May 29, 2022|url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20220529163550/https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archivedate=May 29, 2022|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref>.
== Overview == The festivals are characterized by the use of tall, rectangular lantern floats called {{ill|Kiriko|ja|切籠|12=draft}} or {{ill|Hōtō|ja|奉燈|12=draft}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} While some kiriko lanterns exceed 10 meters in height, most are between 4 to 6 meters and are carried by groups of around 20 people.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} In some areas, they are also referred to as "Oakashii" (御明かし). The festivals typically feature processions through town streets to a temporary shrine (often located near water or in open spaces) before returning to the main shrine.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Some festivals include sea crossings during their processions.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
Held primarily from July to September as summer or autumn festivals, they occur at about 200 locations throughout the peninsula.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="sankeiphoto"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Many of these festivals originated with prayers for the prevention of epidemics<ref name="bluesignal"/> or for bountiful harvests and fishing.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== History == The origins of the Kiriko Matsuri are partly rooted in oral traditions, with limited documentation. One of the earliest references to kiriko can be found in the 1647 (Shōhō 3) festival records of {{ill|Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|輪島住吉神社|12=draft}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> The {{ill|Abare Matsuri|ja|あばれ祭|12=draft}} of {{ill|Ushitsu|ja|宇出津|12=draft}}, held at {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社 (能登町)|12=draft}}, is said to have started during the Kanbun era (1661–1673), although this is debated.<ref name="asa20220529"/> The festivals are generally believed to have originated during the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}} and derive either from the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭|12=draft}} in Kyoto or from midsummer Shinto rites.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
In December 1997 (Heisei 9), the "Noto Kiriko Matsuri" was designated as a {{ill|Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財|12=draft}} by the national government.<ref>{{Cultural Heritage Online|161401|Noto's Kiriko Festivals}}</ref> In 2015, the festivals were recognized as part of the Japan Heritage program under the title "Islands of Light: Noto Peninsula's Passionate Kiriko Festivals."<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref>[http://www.bunka.go.jp/seisaku/bunkazai/nihon_isan/ichiran.html List of Japan Heritage Sites] - Agency for Cultural Affairs</ref>
== Kiriko == [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (13).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lanterns on display at the Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]] [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (6).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lantern with auspicious characters]]
Kiriko lanterns are typically tall, rectangular {{ill|Andon|ja|行灯|12=draft}} with "auspicious characters" (吉祥文字) representing prayers and wishes inscribed on the front.<ref name="bluesignal"/> The back often features elaborate paintings, such as warrior imagery or landscapes.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} The lanterns are topped with a {{ill|Kirizuma roof|ja|切妻屋根|12=draft}} and adorned with decorations like paper streamers and {{ill|Chōchin|ja|提灯|12=draft}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} Designs and dimensions vary by region, with some kiriko resembling the curved shapes of {{ill|Nebuta|ja|青森ねぶた|12=draft}} floats.
It is believed that kiriko originated as small, hand-held bamboo sticks with paper lanterns ("Sasa Kiriko") and gradually grew larger, reaching their current size by the late Edo period.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> However, depopulation has led to a shortage of carriers in some regions, prompting adjustments like rescheduling festivals to weekends.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Major Kiriko Festivals == Dates vary annually. Listed below are notable examples:
; Nanao * {{ill|Mutomachi Festival|ja|互市祭|12=draft}} - Early July * {{ill|Nanao Gion Matsuri|ja|七尾祇園祭|12=draft}} - Early July * {{ill|Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|ja|向田の火祭り|12=draft}} - Last Saturday of July * {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|12=draft}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}}
; Wajima * Wajima Taisai - August 22–25<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}}
; Suzu * {{ill|Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|ja|飯田町燈籠山祭り|12=draft}} - Late July * {{ill|Hōraku Matsuri|ja|宝立七夕キリコ祭り|12=draft}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Kiriko Festival Facilities == === Wajima Kiriko Art Museum === {{Main|Wajima Kiriko Art Museum}} The museum in {{ill|Wajima|ja|輪島市|12=draft}} showcases 31 kiriko lanterns and explains the festivals.<ref name="asa20220529"/> Opened in 2015, it serves as a cultural hub for the events.<ref>[https://wajima-kiriko.com/ Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
{{Redirect|キリコ祭り|小惑星|キリコ祭り (小惑星)}} {{参照方法|date=2022年12月}} [[File:Noto Yosakoi Wakura 01.jpg|thumb|right|300px|七尾市で行われる石崎奉燈祭]]
'''能登キリコ祭り'''（のとキリコまつり）は、[[石川県]]の[[能登半島]]各地で行われる[[祭|祭り]]である<ref name="bluesignal">{{Cite journal|和書|url = https://www.westjr.co.jp/company/info/issue/bsignal/19_vol_185/issue/01.html|title = 日本遺産を訪ねる西への旅 神をいざなう能登の祭り|journal = Blue Signal|publisher = [[西日本旅客鉄道]]|date = 2019-07|issue = 2019年7月号|accessdate = 2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="sankeiphoto">{{Cite web|和書|url = https://www.sankei.com/photo/story/news/191006/sty1910060001-n1.html|title = 【美しきにっぽん】夢幻 漆黒照らす燈籠 能登半島・キリコ祭り|website = 産経フォト|date = 2019-10-02|accessdate = 2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="asa20220529">{{Cite news|url = https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|title = 能登の祭りの代表格「キリコ」 土屋太鳳さん、自衛隊も担ぐ|newspaper = [[朝日新聞デジタル]]|archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20220529163550/https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|date = 2022-05-29|archivedate = 2022-05-29|accessdate = 2022-12-30}}</ref>。
== 概要 == 切籠（キリコ）または奉燈（ほうとう）と呼ばれる切子[[灯籠]]を使うことを特徴とするものである<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}。[[高さ]]は10[[メートル]]を超える巨大なキリコもあるが、4メートルから6メートルのものが主流で20人前後が担ぎ手としてキリコを担ぐ<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}}。地域によって御明かし（おあかし）とも呼ばれる。市街地を練り歩き、[[御旅所]]（多くは水辺や広場）に[[渡御]]し[[神社]]に戻るのが一般的で{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}、祭りによっては[[海面|海上]]を渡御するものもある{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}。
主に[[7月]]から[[9月]]に掛けて夏祭り・秋祭りとして{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134-135}}、能登半島の約200か所で行われており<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="sankeiphoto"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}、[[疫病]]退散<ref name="bluesignal"/>または[[大漁]]・[[豊作]]<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>を願って始まったとされるものが多い。
== 歴史 == <!-- 『まれ』についての具体的な記述は、検証不可能な記述として一部削除していますのでご了承願います。また、マークアップした部分についての出典提示が今後もない場合は、記述を削除します --> キリコ祭りの歴史は伝承の中にあるものがあり、文献による記録はそう多くないが、[[1647年]]（[[正保]]3年）の輪島住吉神社の祭礼定書にキリコのことが書かれている<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>。{{要出典範囲|宇出津・八坂神社のキリコ祭り（あばれ祭り）については[[寛文]]年間（1661年-1673年）に桜井源五という人が始めたと伝承されているが、これについては異論もある|date=2022年12月}}。祭りは[[江戸時代]]の発祥が起源とされ{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}、由来は祭りによって異なるが[[京都]]の[[祇園祭]]に由来または夏超しの[[神事]]に由来するものに分けられる<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}。
[[1997年]]（[[平成]]9年）[[12月4日]]、「能登のキリコ祭り」として国の「[[記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財]]」に選択された<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|161401|能登のキリコ祭り}}</ref>。
[[2015年]]（平成27年）に放送されたNHK[[連続テレビ小説]]『[[まれ]]』では、ヒロイン役の[[土屋太鳳]]が劇中でキリコを担いで祭りに参加している<ref name="asa20220529"/>。また、同年[[4月24日]]には、「灯り舞う半島 能登 ～熱狂のキリコ祭り～」の名称で[[文化庁]]による[[日本遺産]]に認定された<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref>[http://www.bunka.go.jp/seisaku/bunkazai/nihon_isan/ichiran.html これまでに認定された「日本遺産」一覧] 文化庁、2018年12月5日</ref>。
== キリコ == [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (13).jpg|thumb|並べられたキリコ（輪島キリコ会館）]] [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (6).jpg|thumb|キリコ吉祥文字]]
キリコ（切籠）は、代表的な形としては背が高い[[直方体]]状の[[行灯]]で<ref name="bluesignal"/>、前面中央部には[[漢字]]3文字で表した「キリコ吉祥文字」と呼ばれる地区ごとの願いや祈りを込めた文字を配し<ref name="bluesignal"/>、「後美人」と呼ばれる背面には様々な絵（[[武者絵]]や[[風景画]]など）が施されている<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}}。上部は[[切妻屋根]]の構造で{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}}、[[提灯]]や[[御幣]]などの飾りが付けられている{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}}。ただ実際のキリコの形や大きさはその地区・地区により様々である{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}}。中には[[青森ねぶた]]にも似た曲線状のものも存在する。
キリコは「笹キリコ」と呼ばれる[[ササ|笹]][[竹]]の先端に[[和紙]]を付けた手で持てる程度のものが始まりとされており<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>、次第に巨大化していき、江戸時代後期には現在のキリコになったとされている<ref name="asa20220529"/>。また、近年は[[過疎|過疎化]]に伴い担ぎ手が不足している地域があり、[[宝立七夕キリコ祭り]]では日程を[[2019年]]（[[令和]]元年）からこれまでの[[8月7日]]の開催日を8月の第1土曜日に変更した例もある<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>。
== 主なキリコ祭り == 記載している期日は年度によって変動がある。 ; [[七尾市]] * 互市祭 - 7月上旬 * [[七尾祇園祭]] - 7月上旬 * [[能登島]][[向田の火祭り]] - 7月最終土曜日 *[[六保祭|六保納涼祭]] - 7月最終土曜日 * なごしの祭り - 7月下旬 * 塩津かがり火恋まつり - 7月第4土曜日 * [[石崎奉燈祭]]<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}} - 8月第1土曜日{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}} * [[新宮納涼祭]] - [[8月14日]]
; [[輪島市]] * 剱地八幡神社大祭（おさよ祭り） - 7月中旬 * 水無月祭り - 7月下旬 * 名舟大祭 - 7月下旬 * 曽々木大祭 - 8月17日 * 輪島大祭（奥津姫神社大祭・重蔵神社大祭<ref name="asa20220529"/>：住吉神社大祭・輪島前神社大祭の総称）<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}} - [[8月22日]]{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}} - [[8月25日]]{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}}
; [[珠洲市]] * [[飯田町燈籠山祭り]]<ref name="bluesignal"/> - 7月下旬 * [[宝立七夕キリコ祭り]]<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="sankeiphoto"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}} - 8月第1土曜日<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>（[[2018年]]までは[[8月7日]]{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}}） * 蛸島キリコ祭り - 9月10日、11日 * 正院キリコ祭り - 9月中旬 * 寺家キリコ祭り - 9月第2土日 * 馬緤キリコ祭り - 10月13、14日
; [[鳳珠郡]][[穴水町]] * 中居キリコ祭り - 7月下旬 * 明千寺キリコ祭り - 8月中旬 * 沖波大漁祭り<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}} - [[8月14日]]・[[8月15日]]{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}} * 大町・川島祭り - 9月中旬 * 前波曳山祭 - 9月中旬
; 鳳珠郡[[能登町]] * [[あばれ祭り|あばれ祭]]<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}}<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url = https://www.ntv.co.jp/showchannel/articles/21271o3nq9agacxvp59j.html|title = 松下洸平が櫻井翔と名店のカルボナーラに挑戦!風間俊介は能登のあばれ祭へ!【1億3000万人のSHOWチャンネル】|website = [[1億3000万人のSHOWチャンネル]]|date = 2022-07-30|accessdate = 2022-12-30}}</ref> - 7月第1金・土曜日{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}} * 藤波キリコ祭り - 7月14日・15日 * 姫どいやさ祭 - 7月第4土・日曜日 * 松波人形キリコ祭り - 7月第4土曜日 * 恋路火祭り - 7月27日 * ござれ祭り - 8月中旬 * にわか祭 - 8月24日 * 波並大祭 - 9月第1金・土曜日 * 柳田大祭 - 9月16日 * 小木袖キリコ祭り - 9月第3土・日曜日
; [[羽咋郡]][[志賀町]] * 大福寺祭 - 8月上旬 * 西海祭り<ref name="bluesignal"/> - 8月14日 * 酒見祭 - 8月下旬 * 富来八朔祭り - 8月下旬 * 福浦祭 - 8月下旬 * 笹波・前浜祭 - 8月下旬
== キリコ祭りを紹介する施設 == [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (3).jpg|thumb|輪島キリコ会館]]
=== 輪島キリコ会館 === {{Main|輪島キリコ会館}}
石川県[[輪島市]]マリンタウンには[[輪島キリコ会館]]がある<ref name="asa20220529"/>。大小のキリコ31基を常設展示し、キリコ祭りを紹介している<ref name="asa20220529"/>。1979年（昭和54年）6月、塚田町3-22-2に前身の能登のお祭り館 キリコ会館が開館した。2015年（平成27年）3月29日、マリンタウンに移転して輪島キリコ会館としてリニューアルオープンした<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://wajima-kiriko.com/news/20150328/ |title=いよいよ明日輪島キリコ会館オープンです！|publisher=輪島キリコ会館|date=2015-03-28|accessdate=2020-08-27}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.notonosho.co.jp/blog/2015/03/29/%E3%82%AD%E3%83%AA%E3%82%B3%E4%BC%9A%E9%A4%A8%E6%9C%AC%E6%97%A5%E3%83%AA%E3%83%8B%E3%83%A5%E3%83%BC%E3%82%A2%E3%83%AB%E3%82%AA%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97%E3%83%B3%EF%BC%81%E7%9F%B3%E5%B7%9D%E7%9C%8C%E3%80%80/ |title=キリコ会館 本日リニューアルオープン|publisher= 輪島ねぶた温泉能登の庄|date=2015-03-29|accessdate=2020-08-27}}</ref>。
=== 和倉温泉お祭り会館 === {{Main|和倉温泉お祭り会館}}
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == {{参照方法|date=2022年12月|section=1}} <!-- リーフレットは検証できる文献とはなりません。脚注引用していない文献は、削除される場合がありますのでご注意願います --> * 北國総合研究所（編）『日本遺産 能登のキリコ祭り』[[北國新聞社]]、2016年 ISBN 978-4-8330-2068-8 * {{Cite book|和書|title = ニッポンを解剖する!金沢 能登図鑑|publisher = [[JTBパブリッシング]]|date = 2018-03-01|isbn = 978-4-533-12465-5|ref = {{sfnref|JTB|2018}} }}
== 関連項目 == * [[キリコ祭り (小惑星)]] * [[和倉温泉お祭り会館]]
== 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Noto Kiriko Matsuri}} * [https://www.hot-ishikawa.jp/feature/kirikomatsuri/top ほっと石川旅ねっと 巨大な灯籠が乱舞 魂をゆさぶる 能登の「キリコ祭り」特集!!] - 石川県観光連盟 * [https://japan-heritage.bunka.go.jp/ja/stories/story004/ 灯（あか）り舞う半島 能登] - 日本遺産ポータルサイト（文化庁） * [http://www.notokiriko.jp/index.html 能登キリコ祭り] - 能登キリコ広報委員会（能登キリコネットワーク)

{{デフォルトソート:のときりこまつり}} [[Category:能登キリコ祭り|*]] [[Category:石川県の祭り]] [[Category:疫病退散]] [[Category:日本遺産]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[能登キリコ祭り]] to [[Noto Kiriko Festivals]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{Redirect|キリコ祭り|小惑星|キリコ祭り (小惑星)}} {{参照方法|date=2022年12月}} [[File:Noto Yosakoi Wakura 01.jpg|thumb|right|300px|七尾市で行われる石崎奉燈祭]]
'''能登キリコ祭り'''（のとキリコまつり）は、[[石川県]]の[[能登半島]]各地で行われる[[祭|祭り]]である<ref name="bluesignal">{{Cite journal|和書|url = https://www.westjr.co.jp/company/info/issue/bsignal/19_vol_185/issue/01.html|title = 日本遺産を訪ねる西への旅 神をいざなう能登の祭り|journal = Blue Signal|publisher = [[西日本旅客鉄道]]|date = 2019-07|issue = 2019年7月号|accessdate = 2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="sankeiphoto">{{Cite web|和書|url = https://www.sankei.com/photo/story/news/191006/sty1910060001-n1.html|title = 【美しきにっぽん】夢幻 漆黒照らす燈籠 能登半島・キリコ祭り|website = 産経フォト|date = 2019-10-02|accessdate = 2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="asa20220529">{{Cite news|url = https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|title = 能登の祭りの代表格「キリコ」 土屋太鳳さん、自衛隊も担ぐ|newspaper = [[朝日新聞デジタル]]|archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20220529163550/https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|date = 2022-05-29|archivedate = 2022-05-29|accessdate = 2022-12-30}}</ref>。
== 概要 == 切籠（キリコ）または奉燈（ほうとう）と呼ばれる切子[[灯籠]]を使うことを特徴とするものである<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}。[[高さ]]は10[[メートル]]を超える巨大なキリコもあるが、4メートルから6メートルのものが主流で20人前後が担ぎ手としてキリコを担ぐ<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}}。地域によって御明かし（おあかし）とも呼ばれる。市街地を練り歩き、[[御旅所]]（多くは水辺や広場）に[[渡御]]し[[神社]]に戻るのが一般的で{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}、祭りによっては[[海面|海上]]を渡御するものもある{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}。
主に[[7月]]から[[9月]]に掛けて夏祭り・秋祭りとして{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134-135}}、能登半島の約200か所で行われており<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="sankeiphoto"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}、[[疫病]]退散<ref name="bluesignal"/>または[[大漁]]・[[豊作]]<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>を願って始まったとされるものが多い。
== 歴史 == <!-- 『まれ』についての具体的な記述は、検証不可能な記述として一部削除していますのでご了承願います。また、マークアップした部分についての出典提示が今後もない場合は、記述を削除します --> キリコ祭りの歴史は伝承の中にあるものがあり、文献による記録はそう多くないが、[[1647年]]（[[正保]]3年）の輪島住吉神社の祭礼定書にキリコのことが書かれている<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>。{{要出典範囲|宇出津・八坂神社のキリコ祭り（あばれ祭り）については[[寛文]]年間（1661年-1673年）に桜井源五という人が始めたと伝承されているが、これについては異論もある|date=2022年12月}}。祭りは[[江戸時代]]の発祥が起源とされ{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}、由来は祭りによって異なるが[[京都]]の[[祇園祭]]に由来または夏超しの[[神事]]に由来するものに分けられる<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}。
[[1997年]]（[[平成]]9年）[[12月4日]]、「能登のキリコ祭り」として国の「[[記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財]]」に選択された<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|161401|能登のキリコ祭り}}</ref>。
[[2015年]]（平成27年）に放送されたNHK[[連続テレビ小説]]『[[まれ]]』では、ヒロイン役の[[土屋太鳳]]が劇中でキリコを担いで祭りに参加している<ref name="asa20220529"/>。また、同年[[4月24日]]には、「灯り舞う半島 能登 ～熱狂のキリコ祭り～」の名称で[[文化庁]]による[[日本遺産]]に認定された<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref>[http://www.bunka.go.jp/seisaku/bunkazai/nihon_isan/ichiran.html これまでに認定された「日本遺産」一覧] 文化庁、2018年12月5日</ref>。
== キリコ == [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (13).jpg|thumb|並べられたキリコ（輪島キリコ会館）]] [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (6).jpg|thumb|キリコ吉祥文字]]
キリコ（切籠）は、代表的な形としては背が高い[[直方体]]状の[[行灯]]で<ref name="bluesignal"/>、前面中央部には[[漢字]]3文字で表した「キリコ吉祥文字」と呼ばれる地区ごとの願いや祈りを込めた文字を配し<ref name="bluesignal"/>、「後美人」と呼ばれる背面には様々な絵（[[武者絵]]や[[風景画]]など）が施されている<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}}。上部は[[切妻屋根]]の構造で{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}}、[[提灯]]や[[御幣]]などの飾りが付けられている{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}}。ただ実際のキリコの形や大きさはその地区・地区により様々である{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}}。中には[[青森ねぶた]]にも似た曲線状のものも存在する。
キリコは「笹キリコ」と呼ばれる[[ササ|笹]][[竹]]の先端に[[和紙]]を付けた手で持てる程度のものが始まりとされており<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>、次第に巨大化していき、江戸時代後期には現在のキリコになったとされている<ref name="asa20220529"/>。また、近年は[[過疎|過疎化]]に伴い担ぎ手が不足している地域があり、[[宝立七夕キリコ祭り]]では日程を[[2019年]]（[[令和]]元年）からこれまでの[[8月7日]]の開催日を8月の第1土曜日に変更した例もある<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>。
== 主なキリコ祭り == 記載している期日は年度によって変動がある。 ; [[七尾市]] * 互市祭 - 7月上旬 * [[七尾祇園祭]] - 7月上旬 * [[能登島]][[向田の火祭り]] - 7月最終土曜日 *[[六保祭|六保納涼祭]] - 7月最終土曜日 * なごしの祭り - 7月下旬 * 塩津かがり火恋まつり - 7月第4土曜日 * [[石崎奉燈祭]]<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}} - 8月第1土曜日{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}} * [[新宮納涼祭]] - [[8月14日]]
; [[輪島市]] * 剱地八幡神社大祭（おさよ祭り） - 7月中旬 * 水無月祭り - 7月下旬 * 名舟大祭 - 7月下旬 * 曽々木大祭 - 8月17日 * 輪島大祭（奥津姫神社大祭・重蔵神社大祭<ref name="asa20220529"/>：住吉神社大祭・輪島前神社大祭の総称）<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}} - [[8月22日]]{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}} - [[8月25日]]{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}}
; [[珠洲市]] * [[飯田町燈籠山祭り]]<ref name="bluesignal"/> - 7月下旬 * [[宝立七夕キリコ祭り]]<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="sankeiphoto"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}} - 8月第1土曜日<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>（[[2018年]]までは[[8月7日]]{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}}） * 蛸島キリコ祭り - 9月10日、11日 * 正院キリコ祭り - 9月中旬 * 寺家キリコ祭り - 9月第2土日 * 馬緤キリコ祭り - 10月13、14日
; [[鳳珠郡]][[穴水町]] * 中居キリコ祭り - 7月下旬 * 明千寺キリコ祭り - 8月中旬 * 沖波大漁祭り<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}} - [[8月14日]]・[[8月15日]]{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}} * 大町・川島祭り - 9月中旬 * 前波曳山祭 - 9月中旬
; 鳳珠郡[[能登町]] * [[あばれ祭り|あばれ祭]]<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}}<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url = https://www.ntv.co.jp/showchannel/articles/21271o3nq9agacxvp59j.html|title = 松下洸平が櫻井翔と名店のカルボナーラに挑戦!風間俊介は能登のあばれ祭へ!【1億3000万人のSHOWチャンネル】|website = [[1億3000万人のSHOWチャンネル]]|date = 2022-07-30|accessdate = 2022-12-30}}</ref> - 7月第1金・土曜日{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}} * 藤波キリコ祭り - 7月14日・15日 * 姫どいやさ祭 - 7月第4土・日曜日 * 松波人形キリコ祭り - 7月第4土曜日 * 恋路火祭り - 7月27日 * ござれ祭り - 8月中旬 * にわか祭 - 8月24日 * 波並大祭 - 9月第1金・土曜日 * 柳田大祭 - 9月16日 * 小木袖キリコ祭り - 9月第3土・日曜日
; [[羽咋郡]][[志賀町]] * 大福寺祭 - 8月上旬 * 西海祭り<ref name="bluesignal"/> - 8月14日 * 酒見祭 - 8月下旬 * 富来八朔祭り - 8月下旬 * 福浦祭 - 8月下旬 * 笹波・前浜祭 - 8月下旬
== キリコ祭りを紹介する施設 == [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (3).jpg|thumb|輪島キリコ会館]]
=== 輪島キリコ会館 === {{Main|輪島キリコ会館}}
石川県[[輪島市]]マリンタウンには[[輪島キリコ会館]]がある<ref name="asa20220529"/>。大小のキリコ31基を常設展示し、キリコ祭りを紹介している<ref name="asa20220529"/>。1979年（昭和54年）6月、塚田町3-22-2に前身の能登のお祭り館 キリコ会館が開館した。2015年（平成27年）3月29日、マリンタウンに移転して輪島キリコ会館としてリニューアルオープンした<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://wajima-kiriko.com/news/20150328/ |title=いよいよ明日輪島キリコ会館オープンです！|publisher=輪島キリコ会館|date=2015-03-28|accessdate=2020-08-27}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.notonosho.co.jp/blog/2015/03/29/%E3%82%AD%E3%83%AA%E3%82%B3%E4%BC%9A%E9%A4%A8%E6%9C%AC%E6%97%A5%E3%83%AA%E3%83%8B%E3%83%A5%E3%83%BC%E3%82%A2%E3%83%AB%E3%82%AA%E3%83%BC%E3%83%97%E3%83%B3%EF%BC%81%E7%9F%B3%E5%B7%9D%E7%9C%8C%E3%80%80/ |title=キリコ会館 本日リニューアルオープン|publisher= 輪島ねぶた温泉能登の庄|date=2015-03-29|accessdate=2020-08-27}}</ref>。
=== 和倉温泉お祭り会館 === {{Main|和倉温泉お祭り会館}}
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == {{参照方法|date=2022年12月|section=1}} <!-- リーフレットは検証できる文献とはなりません。脚注引用していない文献は、削除される場合がありますのでご注意願います --> * 北國総合研究所（編）『日本遺産 能登のキリコ祭り』[[北國新聞社]]、2016年 ISBN 978-4-8330-2068-8 * {{Cite book|和書|title = ニッポンを解剖する!金沢 能登図鑑|publisher = [[JTBパブリッシング]]|date = 2018-03-01|isbn = 978-4-533-12465-5|ref = {{sfnref|JTB|2018}} }}
== 関連項目 == * [[キリコ祭り (小惑星)]] * [[和倉温泉お祭り会館]]
== 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Noto Kiriko Matsuri}} * [https://www.hot-ishikawa.jp/feature/kirikomatsuri/top ほっと石川旅ねっと 巨大な灯籠が乱舞 魂をゆさぶる 能登の「キリコ祭り」特集!!] - 石川県観光連盟 * [https://japan-heritage.bunka.go.jp/ja/stories/story004/ 灯（あか）り舞う半島 能登] - 日本遺産ポータルサイト（文化庁） * [http://www.notokiriko.jp/index.html 能登キリコ祭り] - 能登キリコ広報委員会（能登キリコネットワーク)

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:能登キリコ祭り]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11612004]] Wikidata name: Noto Kiriko Matsuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Redirect|Noto Kiriko Matsuri|asteroid|Kiriko Matsuri (asteroid)}} {{Referencing style|date=December 2022}} [[File:Noto Yosakoi Wakura 01.jpg|thumb|right|300px|The {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|qq=|1=Issaki Hōtō Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585601}} held in [[:en:Nanao, Ishikawa|Nanao]].]]
'''Noto Kiriko Matsuri''' (能登キリコ祭り) are festivals held across the [[:en:Noto Peninsula|Noto Peninsula]] in [[:en:Ishikawa Prefecture|Ishikawa Prefecture]].<ref name="bluesignal">{{Cite journal|title=Travel West to Japan Heritage: Noto's Festivals that Invite the Gods|journal=Blue Signal|publisher=[[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]]|date=July 2019|issue=July 2019|url=https://www.westjr.co.jp/company/info/issue/bsignal/19_vol_185/issue/01.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="sankeiphoto">{{Cite web|title=The Sublime Beauty of Japan: Lantern Festivals on the Noto Peninsula|website=Sankei Photo|date=October 2, 2019|url=https://www.sankei.com/photo/story/news/191006/sty1910060001-n1.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="asa20220529">{{Cite news|title=Noto's Iconic Kiriko Festivals: Taō Tsuchiya and the Self-Defense Forces Join In|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun Digital|date=May 29, 2022|url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20220529163550/https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archivedate=May 29, 2022|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref>.
== Overview == The festivals are characterized by the use of tall, rectangular lantern floats called {{ill|Kiriko|ja|切籠|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiriko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Hōtō|ja|奉燈|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōtō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} While some kiriko lanterns exceed 10 meters in height, most are between 4 to 6 meters and are carried by groups of around 20 people.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} In some areas, they are also referred to as "Oakashii" (御明かし). The festivals typically feature processions through town streets to a temporary shrine (often located near water or in open spaces) before returning to the main shrine.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Some festivals include sea crossings during their processions.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
Held primarily from July to September as summer or autumn festivals, they occur at about 200 locations throughout the peninsula.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="sankeiphoto"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Many of these festivals originated with prayers for the prevention of epidemics<ref name="bluesignal"/> or for bountiful harvests and fishing.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== History == The origins of the Kiriko Matsuri are partly rooted in oral traditions, with limited documentation. One of the earliest references to kiriko can be found in the 1647 (Shōhō 3) festival records of {{ill|Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|輪島住吉神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> The {{ill|Abare Matsuri|ja|あばれ祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abare Matsuri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Abare Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4663470}} of {{ill|Ushitsu|ja|宇出津|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ushitsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, held at {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社 (能登町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasaka Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, is said to have started during the Kanbun era (1661–1673), although this is debated.<ref name="asa20220529"/> The festivals are generally believed to have originated during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and derive either from the [[:en:Gion Matsuri|Gion Matsuri]] in Kyoto or from midsummer Shinto rites.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
In December 1997 (Heisei 9), the "Noto Kiriko Matsuri" was designated as a {{ill|Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|qq=|1=Intangible Folk Cultural Property that need measures such as documentation of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642584}} by the national government.<ref>{{Cultural Heritage Online|161401|Noto's Kiriko Festivals}}</ref> In 2015, the festivals were recognized as part of the Japan Heritage program under the title "Islands of Light: Noto Peninsula's Passionate Kiriko Festivals."<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref>[http://www.bunka.go.jp/seisaku/bunkazai/nihon_isan/ichiran.html List of Japan Heritage Sites] - Agency for Cultural Affairs</ref>
== Kiriko == [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (13).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lanterns on display at the Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]] [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (6).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lantern with auspicious characters]]
Kiriko lanterns are typically tall, rectangular {{ill|Andon|ja|行灯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon|qq=|1=andon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626165}} with "auspicious characters" (吉祥文字) representing prayers and wishes inscribed on the front.<ref name="bluesignal"/> The back often features elaborate paintings, such as warrior imagery or landscapes.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} The lanterns are topped with a [[:en:Gable roof|Kirizuma roof]] and adorned with decorations like paper streamers and {{ill|Chōchin|ja|提灯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōchin|qq=|1=chōchin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15992049}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} Designs and dimensions vary by region, with some kiriko resembling the curved shapes of [[:en:Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|Nebuta]] floats.
It is believed that kiriko originated as small, hand-held bamboo sticks with paper lanterns ("Sasa Kiriko") and gradually grew larger, reaching their current size by the late Edo period.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> However, depopulation has led to a shortage of carriers in some regions, prompting adjustments like rescheduling festivals to weekends.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Major Kiriko Festivals == Dates vary annually. Listed below are notable examples:
; Nanao * {{ill|Mutomachi Festival|ja|互市祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutomachi Festival|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - Early July * {{ill|Nanao Gion Matsuri|ja|七尾祇園祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanao Gion Matsuri|qq=|1=Nanao Gion Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353501}} - Early July * {{ill|Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|ja|向田の火祭り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|qq=}} - Last Saturday of July * {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|qq=|1=Issaki Hōtō Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585601}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}}
; Wajima * Wajima Taisai - August 22–25<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}}
; Suzu * {{ill|Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|ja|飯田町燈籠山祭り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|qq=|1=Iida-machi Toroyama Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666571}} - Late July * {{ill|Hōraku Matsuri|ja|宝立七夕キリコ祭り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraku Matsuri|qq=|1=Hōraku Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452663}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Kiriko Festival Facilities == === Wajima Kiriko Art Museum === {{Main|Wajima Kiriko Art Museum}} The museum in [[:en:Wajima, Ishikawa|Wajima]] showcases 31 kiriko lanterns and explains the festivals.<ref name="asa20220529"/> Opened in 2015, it serves as a cultural hub for the events.<ref>[https://wajima-kiriko.com/ Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:能登キリコ祭り]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11612004]] Wikidata name: Noto Kiriko Matsuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Redirect|Noto Kiriko Matsuri|asteroid|Kiriko Matsuri (asteroid)}} {{Referencing style|date=December 2022}} [[File:Noto Yosakoi Wakura 01.jpg|thumb|right|300px|The {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|qq=|1=Issaki Hōtō Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585601}} held in [[:en:Nanao, Ishikawa|Nanao]].]]
'''Noto Kiriko Matsuri''' (能登キリコ祭り) are festivals held across the [[:en:Noto Peninsula|Noto Peninsula]] in [[:en:Ishikawa Prefecture|Ishikawa Prefecture]].<ref name="bluesignal">{{Cite journal|title=Travel West to Japan Heritage: Noto's Festivals that Invite the Gods|journal=Blue Signal|publisher=[[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]]|date=July 2019|issue=July 2019|url=https://www.westjr.co.jp/company/info/issue/bsignal/19_vol_185/issue/01.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="sankeiphoto">{{Cite web|title=The Sublime Beauty of Japan: Lantern Festivals on the Noto Peninsula|website=Sankei Photo|date=October 2, 2019|url=https://www.sankei.com/photo/story/news/191006/sty1910060001-n1.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="asa20220529">{{Cite news|title=Noto's Iconic Kiriko Festivals: Taō Tsuchiya and the Self-Defense Forces Join In|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun Digital|date=May 29, 2022|url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20220529163550/https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archivedate=May 29, 2022|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref>.
== Overview == The festivals are characterized by the use of tall, rectangular lantern floats called {{ill|Kiriko|ja|切籠|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiriko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Hōtō|ja|奉燈|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōtō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} While some kiriko lanterns exceed 10 meters in height, most are between 4 to 6 meters and are carried by groups of around 20 people.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} In some areas, they are also referred to as "Oakashii" (御明かし). The festivals typically feature processions through town streets to a temporary shrine (often located near water or in open spaces) before returning to the main shrine.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Some festivals include sea crossings during their processions.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
Held primarily from July to September as summer or autumn festivals, they occur at about 200 locations throughout the peninsula.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="sankeiphoto"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Many of these festivals originated with prayers for the prevention of epidemics<ref name="bluesignal"/> or for bountiful harvests and fishing.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== History == The origins of the Kiriko Matsuri are partly rooted in oral traditions, with limited documentation. One of the earliest references to kiriko can be found in the 1647 (Shōhō 3) festival records of {{ill|Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|輪島住吉神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> The {{ill|Abare Matsuri|ja|あばれ祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abare Matsuri|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Abare Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4663470}} of {{ill|Ushitsu|ja|宇出津|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ushitsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, held at {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社 (能登町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasaka Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, is said to have started during the Kanbun era (1661–1673), although this is debated.<ref name="asa20220529"/> The festivals are generally believed to have originated during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and derive either from the [[:en:Gion Matsuri|Gion Matsuri]] in Kyoto or from midsummer Shinto rites.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
In December 1997 (Heisei 9), the "Noto Kiriko Matsuri" was designated as a {{ill|Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|qq=|1=Intangible Folk Cultural Property that need measures such as documentation of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642584}} by the national government.<ref>{{Cultural Heritage Online|161401|Noto's Kiriko Festivals}}</ref> In 2015, the festivals were recognized as part of the Japan Heritage program under the title "Islands of Light: Noto Peninsula's Passionate Kiriko Festivals."<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref>[http://www.bunka.go.jp/seisaku/bunkazai/nihon_isan/ichiran.html List of Japan Heritage Sites] - Agency for Cultural Affairs</ref>
== Kiriko == [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (13).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lanterns on display at the Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]] [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (6).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lantern with auspicious characters]]
Kiriko lanterns are typically tall, rectangular {{ill|Andon|ja|行灯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon|qq=|1=andon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626165}} with "auspicious characters" (吉祥文字) representing prayers and wishes inscribed on the front.<ref name="bluesignal"/> The back often features elaborate paintings, such as warrior imagery or landscapes.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} The lanterns are topped with a [[:en:Gable roof|Kirizuma roof]] and adorned with decorations like paper streamers and {{ill|Chōchin|ja|提灯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōchin|qq=|1=chōchin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15992049}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} Designs and dimensions vary by region, with some kiriko resembling the curved shapes of [[:en:Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|Nebuta]] floats.
It is believed that kiriko originated as small, hand-held bamboo sticks with paper lanterns ("Sasa Kiriko") and gradually grew larger, reaching their current size by the late Edo period.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> However, depopulation has led to a shortage of carriers in some regions, prompting adjustments like rescheduling festivals to weekends.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Major Kiriko Festivals == Dates vary annually. Listed below are notable examples:
; Nanao * {{ill|Mutomachi Festival|ja|互市祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutomachi Festival|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - Early July * {{ill|Nanao Gion Matsuri|ja|七尾祇園祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanao Gion Matsuri|qq=|1=Nanao Gion Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353501}} - Early July * {{ill|Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|ja|向田の火祭り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|qq=}} - Last Saturday of July * {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|qq=|1=Issaki Hōtō Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585601}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}}
; Wajima * Wajima Taisai - August 22–25<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}}
; Suzu * {{ill|Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|ja|飯田町燈籠山祭り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|qq=|1=Iida-machi Toroyama Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666571}} - Late July * {{ill|Hōraku Matsuri|ja|宝立七夕キリコ祭り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraku Matsuri|qq=|1=Hōraku Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452663}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Kiriko Festival Facilities == === Wajima Kiriko Art Museum === {{Main|Wajima Kiriko Art Museum}} The museum in [[:en:Wajima, Ishikawa|Wajima]] showcases 31 kiriko lanterns and explains the festivals.<ref name="asa20220529"/> Opened in 2015, it serves as a cultural hub for the events.<ref>[https://wajima-kiriko.com/ Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]</ref>

'''Noto Kiriko Matsuri''' (能登キリコ祭り) are festivals held across the [[:en:Noto Peninsula|Noto Peninsula]] in [[:en:Ishikawa Prefecture|Ishikawa Prefecture]].<ref name="bluesignal">{{Cite journal|title=Travel West to Japan Heritage: Noto's Festivals that Invite the Gods|journal=Blue Signal|publisher=[[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]]|date=July 2019|issue=July 2019|url=https://www.westjr.co.jp/company/info/issue/bsignal/19_vol_185/issue/01.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="sankeiphoto">{{Cite web|title=The Sublime Beauty of Japan: Lantern Festivals on the Noto Peninsula|website=Sankei Photo|date=October 2, 2019|url=https://www.sankei.com/photo/story/news/191006/sty1910060001-n1.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="asa20220529">{{Cite news|title=Noto's Iconic Kiriko Festivals: Taō Tsuchiya and the Self-Defense Forces Join In|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun Digital|date=May 29, 2022|url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20220529163550/https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archivedate=May 29, 2022|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref>.
== Overview == The festivals are characterized by the use of tall, rectangular lantern floats called {{ill|Kiriko|ja|切籠|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiriko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Hōtō|ja|奉燈|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōtō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} While some kiriko lanterns exceed 10 meters in height, most are between 4 to 6 meters and are carried by groups of around 20 people.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} In some areas, they are also referred to as "Oakashii" (御明かし). The festivals typically feature processions through town streets to a temporary shrine (often located near water or in open spaces) before returning to the main shrine.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Some festivals include sea crossings during their processions.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
Held primarily from July to September as summer or autumn festivals, they occur at about 200 locations throughout the peninsula.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="sankeiphoto"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Many of these festivals originated with prayers for the prevention of epidemics<ref name="bluesignal"/> or for bountiful harvests and fishing.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== History == The origins of the Kiriko Matsuri are partly rooted in oral traditions, with limited documentation. One of the earliest references to kiriko can be found in the 1647 (Shōhō 3) festival records of {{ill|Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|輪島住吉神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> The {{ill|Abare Matsuri|ja|あばれ祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abare Matsuri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Abare Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4663470}} of {{ill|Ushitsu|ja|宇出津|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ushitsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, held at {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社 (能登町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasaka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, is said to have started during the Kanbun era (1661–1673), although this is debated.<ref name="asa20220529"/> The festivals are generally believed to have originated during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and derive either from the [[:en:Gion Matsuri|Gion Matsuri]] in Kyoto or from midsummer Shinto rites.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
In December 1997 (Heisei 9), the "Noto Kiriko Matsuri" was designated as a {{ill|Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|1=Intangible Folk Cultural Property that need measures such as documentation of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642584}} by the national government.<ref>{{Cultural Heritage Online|161401|Noto's Kiriko Festivals}}</ref> In 2015, the festivals were recognized as part of the Japan Heritage program under the title "Islands of Light: Noto Peninsula's Passionate Kiriko Festivals."<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref>[http://www.bunka.go.jp/seisaku/bunkazai/nihon_isan/ichiran.html List of Japan Heritage Sites] - Agency for Cultural Affairs</ref>
== Kiriko == [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (13).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lanterns on display at the Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]] [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (6).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lantern with auspicious characters]]
Kiriko lanterns are typically tall, rectangular {{ill|Andon|ja|行灯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon|1=andon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626165}} with "auspicious characters" (吉祥文字) representing prayers and wishes inscribed on the front.<ref name="bluesignal"/> The back often features elaborate paintings, such as warrior imagery or landscapes.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} The lanterns are topped with a [[:en:Gable roof|Kirizuma roof]] and adorned with decorations like paper streamers and {{ill|Chōchin|ja|提灯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōchin|1=chōchin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15992049}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} Designs and dimensions vary by region, with some kiriko resembling the curved shapes of [[:en:Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|Nebuta]] floats.
It is believed that kiriko originated as small, hand-held bamboo sticks with paper lanterns ("Sasa Kiriko") and gradually grew larger, reaching their current size by the late Edo period.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> However, depopulation has led to a shortage of carriers in some regions, prompting adjustments like rescheduling festivals to weekends.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Major Kiriko Festivals == Dates vary annually. Listed below are notable examples:
; Nanao * {{ill|Mutomachi Festival|ja|互市祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutomachi Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - Early July * {{ill|Nanao Gion Matsuri|ja|七尾祇園祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanao Gion Matsuri|1=Nanao Gion Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353501}} - Early July * {{ill|Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|ja|向田の火祭り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|qq=}} - Last Saturday of July * {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|1=Issaki Hōtō Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585601}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}}
; Wajima * Wajima Taisai - August 22–25<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}}
; Suzu * {{ill|Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|ja|飯田町燈籠山祭り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|1=Iida-machi Toroyama Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666571}} - Late July * {{ill|Hōraku Matsuri|ja|宝立七夕キリコ祭り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraku Matsuri|1=Hōraku Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452663}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:能登キリコ祭り]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11612004]] Wikidata name: Noto Kiriko Matsuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Redirect|Noto Kiriko Matsuri|asteroid|Kiriko Matsuri (asteroid)}} {{Referencing style|date=December 2022}} [[File:Noto Yosakoi Wakura 01.jpg|thumb|right|300px|The {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|1=Issaki Hōtō Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585601}} held in [[:en:Nanao, Ishikawa|Nanao]].]]
'''Noto Kiriko Matsuri''' (能登キリコ祭り) are festivals held across the [[:en:Noto Peninsula|Noto Peninsula]] in [[:en:Ishikawa Prefecture|Ishikawa Prefecture]].<ref name="bluesignal">{{Cite journal|title=Travel West to Japan Heritage: Noto's Festivals that Invite the Gods|journal=Blue Signal|publisher=[[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]]|date=July 2019|issue=July 2019|url=https://www.westjr.co.jp/company/info/issue/bsignal/19_vol_185/issue/01.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="sankeiphoto">{{Cite web|title=The Sublime Beauty of Japan: Lantern Festivals on the Noto Peninsula|website=Sankei Photo|date=October 2, 2019|url=https://www.sankei.com/photo/story/news/191006/sty1910060001-n1.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="asa20220529">{{Cite news|title=Noto's Iconic Kiriko Festivals: Taō Tsuchiya and the Self-Defense Forces Join In|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun Digital|date=May 29, 2022|url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20220529163550/https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archivedate=May 29, 2022|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref>.
== Overview == The festivals are characterized by the use of tall, rectangular lantern floats called {{ill|Kiriko|ja|切籠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiriko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Hōtō|ja|奉燈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōtō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} While some kiriko lanterns exceed 10 meters in height, most are between 4 to 6 meters and are carried by groups of around 20 people.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} In some areas, they are also referred to as "Oakashii" (御明かし). The festivals typically feature processions through town streets to a temporary shrine (often located near water or in open spaces) before returning to the main shrine.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Some festivals include sea crossings during their processions.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
Held primarily from July to September as summer or autumn festivals, they occur at about 200 locations throughout the peninsula.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="sankeiphoto"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Many of these festivals originated with prayers for the prevention of epidemics<ref name="bluesignal"/> or for bountiful harvests and fishing.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== History == The origins of the Kiriko Matsuri are partly rooted in oral traditions, with limited documentation. One of the earliest references to kiriko can be found in the 1647 (Shōhō 3) festival records of {{ill|Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|輪島住吉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> The {{ill|Abare Matsuri|ja|あばれ祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abare Matsuri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Abare Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4663470}} of {{ill|Ushitsu|ja|宇出津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ushitsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, held at {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社 (能登町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasaka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, is said to have started during the Kanbun era (1661–1673), although this is debated.<ref name="asa20220529"/> The festivals are generally believed to have originated during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and derive either from the [[:en:Gion Matsuri|Gion Matsuri]] in Kyoto or from midsummer Shinto rites.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
In December 1997 (Heisei 9), the "Noto Kiriko Matsuri" was designated as a {{ill|Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|1=Intangible Folk Cultural Property that need measures such as documentation of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642584}} by the national government.<ref>{{Cultural Heritage Online|161401|Noto's Kiriko Festivals}}</ref> In 2015, the festivals were recognized as part of the Japan Heritage program under the title "Islands of Light: Noto Peninsula's Passionate Kiriko Festivals."<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref>[http://www.bunka.go.jp/seisaku/bunkazai/nihon_isan/ichiran.html List of Japan Heritage Sites] - Agency for Cultural Affairs</ref>
== Kiriko == [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (13).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lanterns on display at the Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]] [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (6).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lantern with auspicious characters]]
Kiriko lanterns are typically tall, rectangular {{ill|Andon|ja|行灯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon|1=andon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626165}} with "auspicious characters" (吉祥文字) representing prayers and wishes inscribed on the front.<ref name="bluesignal"/> The back often features elaborate paintings, such as warrior imagery or landscapes.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} The lanterns are topped with a [[:en:Gable roof|Kirizuma roof]] and adorned with decorations like paper streamers and {{ill|Chōchin|ja|提灯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōchin|1=chōchin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15992049}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} Designs and dimensions vary by region, with some kiriko resembling the curved shapes of [[:en:Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|Nebuta]] floats.
It is believed that kiriko originated as small, hand-held bamboo sticks with paper lanterns ("Sasa Kiriko") and gradually grew larger, reaching their current size by the late Edo period.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> However, depopulation has led to a shortage of carriers in some regions, prompting adjustments like rescheduling festivals to weekends.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Major Kiriko Festivals == Dates vary annually. Listed below are notable examples:
; Nanao * {{ill|Mutomachi Festival|ja|互市祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutomachi Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - Early July * {{ill|Nanao Gion Matsuri|ja|七尾祇園祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanao Gion Matsuri|1=Nanao Gion Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353501}} - Early July * {{ill|Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|ja|向田の火祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|qq=}} - Last Saturday of July * {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|1=Issaki Hōtō Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585601}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}}
; Wajima * Wajima Taisai - August 22–25<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}}
; Suzu * {{ill|Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|ja|飯田町燈籠山祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|1=Iida-machi Toroyama Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666571}} - Late July * {{ill|Hōraku Matsuri|ja|宝立七夕キリコ祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraku Matsuri|1=Hōraku Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452663}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Kiriko Festival Facilities == === Wajima Kiriko Art Museum === {{Main|Wajima Kiriko Art Museum}} The museum in [[:en:Wajima, Ishikawa|Wajima]] showcases 31 kiriko lanterns and explains the festivals.<ref name="asa20220529"/> Opened in 2015, it serves as a cultural hub for the events.<ref>[https://wajima-kiriko.com/ Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=Nippon Dissected: Kanazawa and Noto Peninsula|publisher=JTB Publishing|date=March 1, 2018|isbn=978-4-533-12465-5|ref={{sfnref|JTB|2018}}}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kiriko Matsuri (asteroid)|ja|キリコ祭り (小惑星)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiriko Matsuri (asteroid)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=91890 Kiriko Matsuri|WD=Q2264843}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Noto Kiriko Matsuri}} * [https://www.hot-ishikawa.jp/feature/kirikomatsuri/top Hot Ishikawa Tourism: Kiriko Matsuri] * [https://japan-heritage.bunka.go.jp/ Islands of Light: Noto's Kiriko Festivals] - Japan Heritage

'''Noto Kiriko Matsuri''' (能登キリコ祭り) are festivals held across the [[:en:Noto Peninsula|Noto Peninsula]] in [[:en:Ishikawa Prefecture|Ishikawa Prefecture]].<ref name="bluesignal">{{Cite journal|title=Travel West to Japan Heritage: Noto's Festivals that Invite the Gods|journal=Blue Signal|publisher=[[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]]|date=July 2019|issue=July 2019|url=https://www.westjr.co.jp/company/info/issue/bsignal/19_vol_185/issue/01.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="sankeiphoto">{{Cite web|title=The Sublime Beauty of Japan: Lantern Festivals on the Noto Peninsula|website=Sankei Photo|date=October 2, 2019|url=https://www.sankei.com/photo/story/news/191006/sty1910060001-n1.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="asa20220529">{{Cite news|title=Noto's Iconic Kiriko Festivals: Taō Tsuchiya and the Self-Defense Forces Join In|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun Digital|date=May 29, 2022|url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20220529163550/https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archivedate=May 29, 2022|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref>.

The festivals typically feature processions through town streets to a temporary shrine (often located near water or in open spaces) before returning to the main shrine.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Some festivals include sea crossings during their processions.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
Held primarily from July to September as summer or autumn festivals, they occur at about 200 locations throughout the peninsula.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="sankeiphoto"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Many of these festivals originated with prayers for the prevention of epidemics<ref name="bluesignal"/> or for bountiful harvests and fishing.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== History == The origins of the Kiriko Matsuri are partly rooted in oral traditions, with limited documentation. One of the earliest references to kiriko can be found in the 1647 (Shōhō 3) festival records of {{ill|Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|輪島住吉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> The {{ill|Abare Matsuri|ja|あばれ祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abare Matsuri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Abare Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4663470}} of {{ill|Ushitsu|ja|宇出津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ushitsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, held at {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社 (能登町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasaka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, is said to have started during the Kanbun era (1661–1673), although this is debated.<ref name="asa20220529"/> The festivals are generally believed to have originated during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and derive either from the [[:en:Gion Matsuri|Gion Matsuri]] in Kyoto or from midsummer Shinto rites.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
In December 1997 (Heisei 9), the "Noto Kiriko Matsuri" was designated as a {{ill|Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|1=Intangible Folk Cultural Property that need measures such as documentation of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642584}} by the national government.<ref>{{Cultural Heritage Online|161401|Noto's Kiriko Festivals}}</ref> In 2015, the festivals were recognized as part of the Japan Heritage program under the title "Islands of Light: Noto Peninsula's Passionate Kiriko Festivals."<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref>[http://www.bunka.go.jp/seisaku/bunkazai/nihon_isan/ichiran.html List of Japan Heritage Sites] - Agency for Cultural Affairs</ref>
== Kiriko == [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (13).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lanterns on display at the Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]] [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (6).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lantern with auspicious characters]]
Kiriko lanterns are typically tall, rectangular {{ill|Andon|ja|行灯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon|1=andon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626165}} with "auspicious characters" (吉祥文字) representing prayers and wishes inscribed on the front.<ref name="bluesignal"/> The back often features elaborate paintings, such as warrior imagery or landscapes.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} The lanterns are topped with a [[:en:Gable roof|Kirizuma roof]] and adorned with decorations like paper streamers and {{ill|Chōchin|ja|提灯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōchin|1=chōchin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15992049}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} Designs and dimensions vary by region, with some kiriko resembling the curved shapes of [[:en:Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|Nebuta]] floats.
It is believed that kiriko originated as small, hand-held bamboo sticks with paper lanterns ("Sasa Kiriko") and gradually grew larger, reaching their current size by the late Edo period.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> However, depopulation has led to a shortage of carriers in some regions, prompting adjustments like rescheduling festivals to weekends.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Major Kiriko Festivals == Dates vary annually. Listed below are notable examples:
; Nanao * {{ill|Mutomachi Festival|ja|互市祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutomachi Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - Early July * {{ill|Nanao Gion Matsuri|ja|七尾祇園祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanao Gion Matsuri|1=Nanao Gion Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353501}} - Early July * {{ill|Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|ja|向田の火祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|qq=}} - Last Saturday of July * {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|1=Issaki Hōtō Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585601}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}}
; Wajima * Wajima Taisai - August 22–25<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}}
; Suzu * {{ill|Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|ja|飯田町燈籠山祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|1=Iida-machi Toroyama Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666571}} - Late July * {{ill|Hōraku Matsuri|ja|宝立七夕キリコ祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraku Matsuri|1=Hōraku Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452663}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Kiriko Festival Facilities == === Wajima Kiriko Art Museum === {{Main|Wajima Kiriko Art Museum}} The museum in [[:en:Wajima, Ishikawa|Wajima]] showcases 31 kiriko lanterns and explains the festivals.<ref name="asa20220529"/> Opened in 2015, it serves as a cultural hub for the events.<ref>[https://wajima-kiriko.com/ Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]</ref>
