Held primarily from July to September as summer or autumn festivals, they occur at about 200 locations throughout the peninsula.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="sankeiphoto"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Many of these festivals originated with prayers for the prevention of epidemics<ref name="bluesignal"/> or for bountiful harvests and fishing.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== History == The origins of the Kiriko Matsuri are partly rooted in oral traditions, with limited documentation. One of the earliest references to kiriko can be found in the 1647 (Shōhō 3) festival records of {{ill|Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|輪島住吉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> The {{ill|Abare Matsuri|ja|あばれ祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abare Matsuri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Abare Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4663470}} of {{ill|Ushitsu|ja|宇出津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ushitsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, held at {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社 (能登町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasaka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, is said to have started during the Kanbun era (1661–1673), although this is debated.<ref name="asa20220529"/> The festivals are generally believed to have originated during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and derive either from the [[:en:Gion Matsuri|Gion Matsuri]] in Kyoto or from midsummer Shinto rites.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
In December 1997 (Heisei 9), the "Noto Kiriko Matsuri" was designated as a {{ill|Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|1=Intangible Folk Cultural Property that need measures such as documentation of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642584}} by the national government.<ref>{{Cultural Heritage Online|161401|Noto's Kiriko Festivals}}</ref> In 2015, the festivals were recognized as part of the Japan Heritage program under the title "Islands of Light: Noto Peninsula's Passionate Kiriko Festivals."<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref>[http://www.bunka.go.jp/seisaku/bunkazai/nihon_isan/ichiran.html List of Japan Heritage Sites] - Agency for Cultural Affairs</ref>
== Kiriko == [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (13).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lanterns on display at the Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]] [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (6).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lantern with auspicious characters]]
Kiriko lanterns are typically tall, rectangular {{ill|Andon|ja|行灯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon|1=andon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626165}} with "auspicious characters" (吉祥文字) representing prayers and wishes inscribed on the front.<ref name="bluesignal"/> The back often features elaborate paintings, such as warrior imagery or landscapes.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} The lanterns are topped with a [[:en:Gable roof|Kirizuma roof]] and adorned with decorations like paper streamers and {{ill|Chōchin|ja|提灯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōchin|1=chōchin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15992049}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} Designs and dimensions vary by region, with some kiriko resembling the curved shapes of [[:en:Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|Nebuta]] floats.
It is believed that kiriko originated as small, hand-held bamboo sticks with paper lanterns ("Sasa Kiriko") and gradually grew larger, reaching their current size by the late Edo period.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> However, depopulation has led to a shortage of carriers in some regions, prompting adjustments like rescheduling festivals to weekends.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Major Kiriko Festivals == Dates vary annually. Listed below are notable examples:
; Nanao * {{ill|Mutomachi Festival|ja|互市祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutomachi Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - Early July * {{ill|Nanao Gion Matsuri|ja|七尾祇園祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanao Gion Matsuri|1=Nanao Gion Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353501}} - Early July * {{ill|Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|ja|向田の火祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|qq=}} - Last Saturday of July * {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|1=Issaki Hōtō Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585601}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}}
; Wajima * Wajima Taisai - August 22–25<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}}
; Suzu * {{ill|Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|ja|飯田町燈籠山祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|1=Iida-machi Toroyama Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666571}} - Late July * {{ill|Hōraku Matsuri|ja|宝立七夕キリコ祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraku Matsuri|1=Hōraku Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452663}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Kiriko Festival Facilities == === Wajima Kiriko Art Museum === {{Main|Wajima Kiriko Art Museum}} The museum in [[:en:Wajima, Ishikawa|Wajima]] showcases 31 kiriko lanterns and explains the festivals.<ref name="asa20220529"/> Opened in 2015, it serves as a cultural hub for the events.<ref>[https://wajima-kiriko.com/ Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:能登キリコ祭り]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11612004]] Wikidata name: Noto Kiriko Matsuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Redirect|Noto Kiriko Matsuri|asteroid|Kiriko Matsuri (asteroid)}} {{Referencing style|date=December 2022}} [[File:Noto Yosakoi Wakura 01.jpg|thumb|right|300px|The {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|1=Issaki Hōtō Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585601}} held in [[:en:Nanao, Ishikawa|Nanao]].]]
'''Noto Kiriko Matsuri''' (能登キリコ祭り) are festivals held across the [[:en:Noto Peninsula|Noto Peninsula]] in [[:en:Ishikawa Prefecture|Ishikawa Prefecture]].<ref name="bluesignal">{{Cite journal|title=Travel West to Japan Heritage: Noto's Festivals that Invite the Gods|journal=Blue Signal|publisher=[[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]]|date=July 2019|issue=July 2019|url=https://www.westjr.co.jp/company/info/issue/bsignal/19_vol_185/issue/01.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="sankeiphoto">{{Cite web|title=The Sublime Beauty of Japan: Lantern Festivals on the Noto Peninsula|website=Sankei Photo|date=October 2, 2019|url=https://www.sankei.com/photo/story/news/191006/sty1910060001-n1.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="asa20220529">{{Cite news|title=Noto's Iconic Kiriko Festivals: Taō Tsuchiya and the Self-Defense Forces Join In|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun Digital|date=May 29, 2022|url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20220529163550/https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archivedate=May 29, 2022|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref>.
== Overview == The festivals are characterized by the use of tall, rectangular lantern floats called {{ill|Kiriko|ja|切籠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiriko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Hōtō|ja|奉燈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōtō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} While some kiriko lanterns exceed 10 meters in height, most are between 4 to 6 meters and are carried by groups of around 20 people.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} In some areas, they are also referred to as "Oakashii" (御明かし). The festivals typically feature processions through town streets to a temporary shrine (often located near water or in open spaces) before returning to the main shrine.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Some festivals include sea crossings during their processions.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
Held primarily from July to September as summer or autumn festivals, they occur at about 200 locations throughout the peninsula.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="sankeiphoto"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Many of these festivals originated with prayers for the prevention of epidemics<ref name="bluesignal"/> or for bountiful harvests and fishing.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== History == The origins of the Kiriko Matsuri are partly rooted in oral traditions, with limited documentation. One of the earliest references to kiriko can be found in the 1647 (Shōhō 3) festival records of {{ill|Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|輪島住吉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> The {{ill|Abare Matsuri|ja|あばれ祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abare Matsuri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Abare Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4663470}} of {{ill|Ushitsu|ja|宇出津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ushitsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, held at {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社 (能登町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasaka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, is said to have started during the Kanbun era (1661–1673), although this is debated.<ref name="asa20220529"/> The festivals are generally believed to have originated during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and derive either from the [[:en:Gion Matsuri|Gion Matsuri]] in Kyoto or from midsummer Shinto rites.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
In December 1997 (Heisei 9), the "Noto Kiriko Matsuri" was designated as a {{ill|Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|1=Intangible Folk Cultural Property that need measures such as documentation of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642584}} by the national government.<ref>{{Cultural Heritage Online|161401|Noto's Kiriko Festivals}}</ref> In 2015, the festivals were recognized as part of the Japan Heritage program under the title "Islands of Light: Noto Peninsula's Passionate Kiriko Festivals."<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref>[http://www.bunka.go.jp/seisaku/bunkazai/nihon_isan/ichiran.html List of Japan Heritage Sites] - Agency for Cultural Affairs</ref>
== Kiriko == [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (13).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lanterns on display at the Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]] [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (6).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lantern with auspicious characters]]
Kiriko lanterns are typically tall, rectangular {{ill|Andon|ja|行灯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon|1=andon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626165}} with "auspicious characters" (吉祥文字) representing prayers and wishes inscribed on the front.<ref name="bluesignal"/> The back often features elaborate paintings, such as warrior imagery or landscapes.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} The lanterns are topped with a [[:en:Gable roof|Kirizuma roof]] and adorned with decorations like paper streamers and {{ill|Chōchin|ja|提灯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōchin|1=chōchin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15992049}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} Designs and dimensions vary by region, with some kiriko resembling the curved shapes of [[:en:Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|Nebuta]] floats.
It is believed that kiriko originated as small, hand-held bamboo sticks with paper lanterns ("Sasa Kiriko") and gradually grew larger, reaching their current size by the late Edo period.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> However, depopulation has led to a shortage of carriers in some regions, prompting adjustments like rescheduling festivals to weekends.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Major Kiriko Festivals == Dates vary annually. Listed below are notable examples:
; Nanao * {{ill|Mutomachi Festival|ja|互市祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutomachi Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - Early July * {{ill|Nanao Gion Matsuri|ja|七尾祇園祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanao Gion Matsuri|1=Nanao Gion Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353501}} - Early July * {{ill|Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|ja|向田の火祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|qq=}} - Last Saturday of July * {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|1=Issaki Hōtō Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585601}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}}
; Wajima * Wajima Taisai - August 22–25<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}}
; Suzu * {{ill|Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|ja|飯田町燈籠山祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|1=Iida-machi Toroyama Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666571}} - Late July * {{ill|Hōraku Matsuri|ja|宝立七夕キリコ祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraku Matsuri|1=Hōraku Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452663}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Kiriko Festival Facilities == === Wajima Kiriko Art Museum === {{Main|Wajima Kiriko Art Museum}} The museum in [[:en:Wajima, Ishikawa|Wajima]] showcases 31 kiriko lanterns and explains the festivals.<ref name="asa20220529"/> Opened in 2015, it serves as a cultural hub for the events.<ref>[https://wajima-kiriko.com/ Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]</ref>

== Major Kiriko Festivals == Dates vary annually. Listed below are notable examples:
; Nanao * {{ill|Mutomachi Festival|ja|互市祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutomachi Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - Early July * {{ill|Nanao Gion Matsuri|ja|七尾祇園祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanao Gion Matsuri|1=Nanao Gion Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353501}} - Early July * {{ill|Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|ja|向田の火祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|qq=}} - Last Saturday of July * {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|1=Issaki Hōtō Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585601}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}}
; Wajima * Wajima Taisai - August 22–25<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}}
; Suzu * {{ill|Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|ja|飯田町燈籠山祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|1=Iida-machi Toroyama Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666571}} - Late July * {{ill|Hōraku Matsuri|ja|宝立七夕キリコ祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraku Matsuri|1=Hōraku Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452663}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Kiriko Festival Facilities == === Wajima Kiriko Art Museum === {{Main|Wajima Kiriko Art Museum}} The museum in [[:en:Wajima, Ishikawa|Wajima]] showcases 31 kiriko lanterns and explains the festivals.<ref name="asa20220529"/> Opened in 2015, it serves as a cultural hub for the events.<ref>[https://wajima-kiriko.com/ Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:能登キリコ祭り]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11612004]] Wikidata name: Noto Kiriko Matsuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Redirect|Noto Kiriko Matsuri|asteroid|Kiriko Matsuri (asteroid)}} {{Referencing style|date=December 2022}} [[File:Noto Yosakoi Wakura 01.jpg|thumb|right|300px|The {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|1=Issaki Hōtō Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585601}} held in [[:en:Nanao, Ishikawa|Nanao]].]]
'''Noto Kiriko Matsuri''' (能登キリコ祭り) are festivals held across the [[:en:Noto Peninsula|Noto Peninsula]] in [[:en:Ishikawa Prefecture|Ishikawa Prefecture]].<ref name="bluesignal">{{Cite journal|title=Travel West to Japan Heritage: Noto's Festivals that Invite the Gods|journal=Blue Signal|publisher=[[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]]|date=July 2019|issue=July 2019|url=https://www.westjr.co.jp/company/info/issue/bsignal/19_vol_185/issue/01.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="sankeiphoto">{{Cite web|title=The Sublime Beauty of Japan: Lantern Festivals on the Noto Peninsula|website=Sankei Photo|date=October 2, 2019|url=https://www.sankei.com/photo/story/news/191006/sty1910060001-n1.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="asa20220529">{{Cite news|title=Noto's Iconic Kiriko Festivals: Taō Tsuchiya and the Self-Defense Forces Join In|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun Digital|date=May 29, 2022|url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20220529163550/https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archivedate=May 29, 2022|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref>.
== Overview == The festivals are characterized by the use of tall, rectangular lantern floats called {{ill|Kiriko|ja|切籠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiriko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Hōtō|ja|奉燈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōtō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} While some kiriko lanterns exceed 10 meters in height, most are between 4 to 6 meters and are carried by groups of around 20 people.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} In some areas, they are also referred to as "Oakashii" (御明かし). The festivals typically feature processions through town streets to a temporary shrine (often located near water or in open spaces) before returning to the main shrine.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Some festivals include sea crossings during their processions.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
Held primarily from July to September as summer or autumn festivals, they occur at about 200 locations throughout the peninsula.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="sankeiphoto"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Many of these festivals originated with prayers for the prevention of epidemics<ref name="bluesignal"/> or for bountiful harvests and fishing.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== History == The origins of the Kiriko Matsuri are partly rooted in oral traditions, with limited documentation. One of the earliest references to kiriko can be found in the 1647 (Shōhō 3) festival records of {{ill|Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|輪島住吉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> The {{ill|Abare Matsuri|ja|あばれ祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abare Matsuri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Abare Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4663470}} of {{ill|Ushitsu|ja|宇出津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ushitsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, held at {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社 (能登町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasaka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, is said to have started during the Kanbun era (1661–1673), although this is debated.<ref name="asa20220529"/> The festivals are generally believed to have originated during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and derive either from the [[:en:Gion Matsuri|Gion Matsuri]] in Kyoto or from midsummer Shinto rites.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
In December 1997 (Heisei 9), the "Noto Kiriko Matsuri" was designated as a {{ill|Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|1=Intangible Folk Cultural Property that need measures such as documentation of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642584}} by the national government.<ref>{{Cultural Heritage Online|161401|Noto's Kiriko Festivals}}</ref> In 2015, the festivals were recognized as part of the Japan Heritage program under the title "Islands of Light: Noto Peninsula's Passionate Kiriko Festivals."<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref>[http://www.bunka.go.jp/seisaku/bunkazai/nihon_isan/ichiran.html List of Japan Heritage Sites] - Agency for Cultural Affairs</ref>
== Kiriko == [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (13).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lanterns on display at the Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]] [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (6).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lantern with auspicious characters]]
Kiriko lanterns are typically tall, rectangular {{ill|Andon|ja|行灯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon|1=andon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626165}} with "auspicious characters" (吉祥文字) representing prayers and wishes inscribed on the front.<ref name="bluesignal"/> The back often features elaborate paintings, such as warrior imagery or landscapes.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} The lanterns are topped with a [[:en:Gable roof|Kirizuma roof]] and adorned with decorations like paper streamers and {{ill|Chōchin|ja|提灯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōchin|1=chōchin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15992049}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} Designs and dimensions vary by region, with some kiriko resembling the curved shapes of [[:en:Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|Nebuta]] floats.
It is believed that kiriko originated as small, hand-held bamboo sticks with paper lanterns ("Sasa Kiriko") and gradually grew larger, reaching their current size by the late Edo period.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> However, depopulation has led to a shortage of carriers in some regions, prompting adjustments like rescheduling festivals to weekends.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Major Kiriko Festivals == Dates vary annually. Listed below are notable examples:
; Nanao * {{ill|Mutomachi Festival|ja|互市祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutomachi Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - Early July * {{ill|Nanao Gion Matsuri|ja|七尾祇園祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanao Gion Matsuri|1=Nanao Gion Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353501}} - Early July * {{ill|Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|ja|向田の火祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|qq=}} - Last Saturday of July * {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|1=Issaki Hōtō Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585601}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}}
; Wajima * Wajima Taisai - August 22–25<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}}
; Suzu * {{ill|Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|ja|飯田町燈籠山祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|1=Iida-machi Toroyama Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666571}} - Late July * {{ill|Hōraku Matsuri|ja|宝立七夕キリコ祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraku Matsuri|1=Hōraku Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452663}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Kiriko Festival Facilities == === Wajima Kiriko Art Museum === {{Main|Wajima Kiriko Art Museum}} The museum in [[:en:Wajima, Ishikawa|Wajima]] showcases 31 kiriko lanterns and explains the festivals.<ref name="asa20220529"/> Opened in 2015, it serves as a cultural hub for the events.<ref>[https://wajima-kiriko.com/ Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]</ref>

== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=Nippon Dissected: Kanazawa and Noto Peninsula|publisher=JTB Publishing|date=March 1, 2018|isbn=978-4-533-12465-5|ref={{sfnref|JTB|2018}}}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kiriko Matsuri (asteroid)|ja|キリコ祭り (小惑星)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiriko Matsuri (asteroid)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=91890 Kiriko Matsuri|WD=Q2264843}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Noto Kiriko Matsuri}} * [https://www.hot-ishikawa.jp/feature/kirikomatsuri/top Hot Ishikawa Tourism: Kiriko Matsuri] * [https://japan-heritage.bunka.go.jp/ Islands of Light: Noto's Kiriko Festivals] - Japan Heritage

== Kiriko == [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (13).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lanterns on display at the Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]] [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (6).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lantern with auspicious characters]]
Kiriko lanterns are typically tall, rectangular {{ill|Andon|ja|行灯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon|1=andon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626165}} with "auspicious characters" (吉祥文字) representing prayers and wishes inscribed on the front.<ref name="bluesignal"/> The back often features elaborate paintings, such as warrior imagery or landscapes.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} The lanterns are topped with a [[:en:Gable roof|Kirizuma roof]] and adorned with decorations like paper streamers and {{ill|Chōchin|ja|提灯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōchin|1=chōchin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15992049}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} Designs and dimensions vary by region, with some kiriko resembling the curved shapes of [[:en:Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|Nebuta]] floats.
It is believed that kiriko originated as small, hand-held bamboo sticks with paper lanterns ("Sasa Kiriko") and gradually grew larger, reaching their current size by the late Edo period.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> However, depopulation has led to a shortage of carriers in some regions, prompting adjustments like rescheduling festivals to weekends.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Major Kiriko Festivals == Dates vary annually. Listed below are notable examples:
; Nanao * {{ill|Mutomachi Festival|ja|互市祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutomachi Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - Early July * {{ill|Nanao Gion Matsuri|ja|七尾祇園祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanao Gion Matsuri|1=Nanao Gion Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353501}} - Early July * {{ill|Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|ja|向田の火祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|qq=}} - Last Saturday of July * {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|1=Issaki Hōtō Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585601}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}}
; Wajima * Wajima Taisai - August 22–25<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}}
; Suzu * {{ill|Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|ja|飯田町燈籠山祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|1=Iida-machi Toroyama Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666571}} - Late July * {{ill|Hōraku Matsuri|ja|宝立七夕キリコ祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraku Matsuri|1=Hōraku Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452663}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:能登キリコ祭り]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11612004]] Wikidata name: Noto Kiriko Matsuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Redirect|Noto Kiriko Matsuri|asteroid|Kiriko Matsuri (asteroid)}} {{Referencing style|date=December 2022}} [[File:Noto Yosakoi Wakura 01.jpg|thumb|right|300px|The {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|1=Issaki Hōtō Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585601}} held in [[:en:Nanao, Ishikawa|Nanao]].]]
'''Noto Kiriko Matsuri''' (能登キリコ祭り) are festivals held across the [[:en:Noto Peninsula|Noto Peninsula]] in [[:en:Ishikawa Prefecture|Ishikawa Prefecture]].<ref name="bluesignal">{{Cite journal|title=Travel West to Japan Heritage: Noto's Festivals that Invite the Gods|journal=Blue Signal|publisher=[[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]]|date=July 2019|issue=July 2019|url=https://www.westjr.co.jp/company/info/issue/bsignal/19_vol_185/issue/01.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="sankeiphoto">{{Cite web|title=The Sublime Beauty of Japan: Lantern Festivals on the Noto Peninsula|website=Sankei Photo|date=October 2, 2019|url=https://www.sankei.com/photo/story/news/191006/sty1910060001-n1.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="asa20220529">{{Cite news|title=Noto's Iconic Kiriko Festivals: Taō Tsuchiya and the Self-Defense Forces Join In|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun Digital|date=May 29, 2022|url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20220529163550/https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archivedate=May 29, 2022|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref>.
== Overview == The festivals are characterized by the use of tall, rectangular lantern floats called {{ill|Kiriko|ja|切籠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiriko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Hōtō|ja|奉燈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōtō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} While some kiriko lanterns exceed 10 meters in height, most are between 4 to 6 meters and are carried by groups of around 20 people.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} In some areas, they are also referred to as "Oakashii" (御明かし). The festivals typically feature processions through town streets to a temporary shrine (often located near water or in open spaces) before returning to the main shrine.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Some festivals include sea crossings during their processions.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
Held primarily from July to September as summer or autumn festivals, they occur at about 200 locations throughout the peninsula.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="sankeiphoto"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Many of these festivals originated with prayers for the prevention of epidemics<ref name="bluesignal"/> or for bountiful harvests and fishing.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== History == The origins of the Kiriko Matsuri are partly rooted in oral traditions, with limited documentation. One of the earliest references to kiriko can be found in the 1647 (Shōhō 3) festival records of {{ill|Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|輪島住吉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> The {{ill|Abare Matsuri|ja|あばれ祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abare Matsuri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Abare Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4663470}} of {{ill|Ushitsu|ja|宇出津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ushitsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, held at {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社 (能登町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasaka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, is said to have started during the Kanbun era (1661–1673), although this is debated.<ref name="asa20220529"/> The festivals are generally believed to have originated during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and derive either from the [[:en:Gion Matsuri|Gion Matsuri]] in Kyoto or from midsummer Shinto rites.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
In December 1997 (Heisei 9), the "Noto Kiriko Matsuri" was designated as a {{ill|Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|1=Intangible Folk Cultural Property that need measures such as documentation of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642584}} by the national government.<ref>{{Cultural Heritage Online|161401|Noto's Kiriko Festivals}}</ref> In 2015, the festivals were recognized as part of the Japan Heritage program under the title "Islands of Light: Noto Peninsula's Passionate Kiriko Festivals."<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref>[http://www.bunka.go.jp/seisaku/bunkazai/nihon_isan/ichiran.html List of Japan Heritage Sites] - Agency for Cultural Affairs</ref>
== Kiriko == [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (13).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lanterns on display at the Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]] [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (6).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lantern with auspicious characters]]
Kiriko lanterns are typically tall, rectangular {{ill|Andon|ja|行灯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon|1=andon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626165}} with "auspicious characters" (吉祥文字) representing prayers and wishes inscribed on the front.<ref name="bluesignal"/> The back often features elaborate paintings, such as warrior imagery or landscapes.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} The lanterns are topped with a [[:en:Gable roof|Kirizuma roof]] and adorned with decorations like paper streamers and {{ill|Chōchin|ja|提灯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōchin|1=chōchin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15992049}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} Designs and dimensions vary by region, with some kiriko resembling the curved shapes of [[:en:Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|Nebuta]] floats.
It is believed that kiriko originated as small, hand-held bamboo sticks with paper lanterns ("Sasa Kiriko") and gradually grew larger, reaching their current size by the late Edo period.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> However, depopulation has led to a shortage of carriers in some regions, prompting adjustments like rescheduling festivals to weekends.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Major Kiriko Festivals == Dates vary annually. Listed below are notable examples:
; Nanao * {{ill|Mutomachi Festival|ja|互市祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutomachi Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - Early July * {{ill|Nanao Gion Matsuri|ja|七尾祇園祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanao Gion Matsuri|1=Nanao Gion Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353501}} - Early July * {{ill|Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|ja|向田の火祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|qq=}} - Last Saturday of July * {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|1=Issaki Hōtō Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585601}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}}
; Wajima * Wajima Taisai - August 22–25<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}}
; Suzu * {{ill|Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|ja|飯田町燈籠山祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|1=Iida-machi Toroyama Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666571}} - Late July * {{ill|Hōraku Matsuri|ja|宝立七夕キリコ祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraku Matsuri|1=Hōraku Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452663}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Kiriko Festival Facilities == === Wajima Kiriko Art Museum === {{Main|Wajima Kiriko Art Museum}} The museum in [[:en:Wajima, Ishikawa|Wajima]] showcases 31 kiriko lanterns and explains the festivals.<ref name="asa20220529"/> Opened in 2015, it serves as a cultural hub for the events.<ref>[https://wajima-kiriko.com/ Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=Nippon Dissected: Kanazawa and Noto Peninsula|publisher=JTB Publishing|date=March 1, 2018|isbn=978-4-533-12465-5|ref={{sfnref|JTB|2018}}}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kiriko Matsuri (asteroid)|ja|キリコ祭り (小惑星)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiriko Matsuri (asteroid)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=91890 Kiriko Matsuri|WD=Q2264843}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Noto Kiriko Matsuri}} * [https://www.hot-ishikawa.jp/feature/kirikomatsuri/top Hot Ishikawa Tourism: Kiriko Matsuri] * [https://japan-heritage.bunka.go.jp/ Islands of Light: Noto's Kiriko Festivals] - Japan Heritage

== History == The origins of the Kiriko Matsuri are partly rooted in oral traditions, with limited documentation. One of the earliest references to kiriko can be found in the 1647 (Shōhō 3) festival records of {{ill|Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|輪島住吉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> The {{ill|Abare Matsuri|ja|あばれ祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abare Matsuri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Abare Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4663470}} of {{ill|Ushitsu|ja|宇出津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ushitsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, held at {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社 (能登町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasaka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, is said to have started during the Kanbun era (1661–1673), although this is debated.<ref name="asa20220529"/> The festivals are generally believed to have originated during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and derive either from the [[:en:Gion Matsuri|Gion Matsuri]] in Kyoto or from midsummer Shinto rites.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
In December 1997 (Heisei 9), the "Noto Kiriko Matsuri" was designated as a {{ill|Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|1=Intangible Folk Cultural Property that need measures such as documentation of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642584}} by the national government.<ref>{{Cultural Heritage Online|161401|Noto's Kiriko Festivals}}</ref> In 2015, the festivals were recognized as part of the Japan Heritage program under the title "Islands of Light: Noto Peninsula's Passionate Kiriko Festivals."<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref>[http://www.bunka.go.jp/seisaku/bunkazai/nihon_isan/ichiran.html List of Japan Heritage Sites] - Agency for Cultural Affairs</ref>
== Kiriko == [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (13).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lanterns on display at the Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]] [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (6).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lantern with auspicious characters]]
Kiriko lanterns are typically tall, rectangular {{ill|Andon|ja|行灯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon|1=andon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626165}} with "auspicious characters" (吉祥文字) representing prayers and wishes inscribed on the front.<ref name="bluesignal"/> The back often features elaborate paintings, such as warrior imagery or landscapes.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} The lanterns are topped with a [[:en:Gable roof|Kirizuma roof]] and adorned with decorations like paper streamers and {{ill|Chōchin|ja|提灯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōchin|1=chōchin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15992049}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} Designs and dimensions vary by region, with some kiriko resembling the curved shapes of [[:en:Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|Nebuta]] floats.
It is believed that kiriko originated as small, hand-held bamboo sticks with paper lanterns ("Sasa Kiriko") and gradually grew larger, reaching their current size by the late Edo period.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> However, depopulation has led to a shortage of carriers in some regions, prompting adjustments like rescheduling festivals to weekends.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Major Kiriko Festivals == Dates vary annually. Listed below are notable examples:
; Nanao * {{ill|Mutomachi Festival|ja|互市祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutomachi Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - Early July * {{ill|Nanao Gion Matsuri|ja|七尾祇園祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanao Gion Matsuri|1=Nanao Gion Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353501}} - Early July * {{ill|Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|ja|向田の火祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|qq=}} - Last Saturday of July * {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|1=Issaki Hōtō Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585601}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}}
; Wajima * Wajima Taisai - August 22–25<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}}
; Suzu * {{ill|Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|ja|飯田町燈籠山祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|1=Iida-machi Toroyama Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666571}} - Late July * {{ill|Hōraku Matsuri|ja|宝立七夕キリコ祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraku Matsuri|1=Hōraku Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452663}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Kiriko Festival Facilities == === Wajima Kiriko Art Museum === {{Main|Wajima Kiriko Art Museum}} The museum in [[:en:Wajima, Ishikawa|Wajima]] showcases 31 kiriko lanterns and explains the festivals.<ref name="asa20220529"/> Opened in 2015, it serves as a cultural hub for the events.<ref>[https://wajima-kiriko.com/ Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]</ref>

== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=Nippon Dissected: Kanazawa and Noto Peninsula|publisher=JTB Publishing|date=March 1, 2018|isbn=978-4-533-12465-5|ref={{sfnref|JTB|2018}}}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kiriko Matsuri (asteroid)|ja|キリコ祭り (小惑星)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiriko Matsuri (asteroid)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=91890 Kiriko Matsuri|WD=Q2264843}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Noto Kiriko Matsuri}} * [https://www.hot-ishikawa.jp/feature/kirikomatsuri/top Hot Ishikawa Tourism: Kiriko Matsuri] * [https://japan-heritage.bunka.go.jp/ Islands of Light: Noto's Kiriko Festivals] - Japan Heritage

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:能登キリコ祭り]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:能登キリコ祭り]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:能登キリコ祭り]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11612004]] Wikidata name: Noto Kiriko Matsuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Redirect|Noto Kiriko Matsuri|asteroid|Kiriko Matsuri (asteroid)}} {{Referencing style|date=December 2022}} [[File:Noto Yosakoi Wakura 01.jpg|thumb|right|300px|The {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|1=Issaki Hōtō Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585601}} held in [[:en:Nanao, Ishikawa|Nanao]].]]
'''Noto Kiriko Matsuri''' (能登キリコ祭り) are festivals held across the [[:en:Noto Peninsula|Noto Peninsula]] in [[:en:Ishikawa Prefecture|Ishikawa Prefecture]].<ref name="bluesignal">{{Cite journal|title=Travel West to Japan Heritage: Noto's Festivals that Invite the Gods|journal=Blue Signal|publisher=[[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]]|date=July 2019|issue=July 2019|url=https://www.westjr.co.jp/company/info/issue/bsignal/19_vol_185/issue/01.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="sankeiphoto">{{Cite web|title=The Sublime Beauty of Japan: Lantern Festivals on the Noto Peninsula|website=Sankei Photo|date=October 2, 2019|url=https://www.sankei.com/photo/story/news/191006/sty1910060001-n1.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="asa20220529">{{Cite news|title=Noto's Iconic Kiriko Festivals: Taō Tsuchiya and the Self-Defense Forces Join In|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun Digital|date=May 29, 2022|url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20220529163550/https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archivedate=May 29, 2022|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref>.
== Overview == The festivals are characterized by the use of tall, rectangular lantern floats called {{ill|Kiriko|ja|切籠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiriko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Hōtō|ja|奉燈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōtō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} While some kiriko lanterns exceed 10 meters in height, most are between 4 to 6 meters and are carried by groups of around 20 people.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} In some areas, they are also referred to as "Oakashii" (御明かし). The festivals typically feature processions through town streets to a temporary shrine (often located near water or in open spaces) before returning to the main shrine.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Some festivals include sea crossings during their processions.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
Held primarily from July to September as summer or autumn festivals, they occur at about 200 locations throughout the peninsula.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="sankeiphoto"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Many of these festivals originated with prayers for the prevention of epidemics<ref name="bluesignal"/> or for bountiful harvests and fishing.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== History == The origins of the Kiriko Matsuri are partly rooted in oral traditions, with limited documentation. One of the earliest references to kiriko can be found in the 1647 (Shōhō 3) festival records of {{ill|Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|輪島住吉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> The {{ill|Abare Matsuri|ja|あばれ祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abare Matsuri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Abare Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4663470}} of {{ill|Ushitsu|ja|宇出津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ushitsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, held at {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社 (能登町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasaka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, is said to have started during the Kanbun era (1661–1673), although this is debated.<ref name="asa20220529"/> The festivals are generally believed to have originated during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and derive either from the [[:en:Gion Matsuri|Gion Matsuri]] in Kyoto or from midsummer Shinto rites.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
In December 1997 (Heisei 9), the "Noto Kiriko Matsuri" was designated as a {{ill|Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|1=Intangible Folk Cultural Property that need measures such as documentation of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642584}} by the national government.<ref>{{Cultural Heritage Online|161401|Noto's Kiriko Festivals}}</ref> In 2015, the festivals were recognized as part of the Japan Heritage program under the title "Islands of Light: Noto Peninsula's Passionate Kiriko Festivals."<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref>[http://www.bunka.go.jp/seisaku/bunkazai/nihon_isan/ichiran.html List of Japan Heritage Sites] - Agency for Cultural Affairs</ref>
== Kiriko == [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (13).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lanterns on display at the Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]] [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (6).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lantern with auspicious characters]]
Kiriko lanterns are typically tall, rectangular {{ill|Andon|ja|行灯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon|1=andon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626165}} with "auspicious characters" (吉祥文字) representing prayers and wishes inscribed on the front.<ref name="bluesignal"/> The back often features elaborate paintings, such as warrior imagery or landscapes.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} The lanterns are topped with a [[:en:Gable roof|Kirizuma roof]] and adorned with decorations like paper streamers and {{ill|Chōchin|ja|提灯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōchin|1=chōchin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15992049}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} Designs and dimensions vary by region, with some kiriko resembling the curved shapes of [[:en:Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|Nebuta]] floats.
It is believed that kiriko originated as small, hand-held bamboo sticks with paper lanterns ("Sasa Kiriko") and gradually grew larger, reaching their current size by the late Edo period.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> However, depopulation has led to a shortage of carriers in some regions, prompting adjustments like rescheduling festivals to weekends.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Major Kiriko Festivals == Dates vary annually. Listed below are notable examples:
; Nanao * {{ill|Mutomachi Festival|ja|互市祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutomachi Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - Early July * {{ill|Nanao Gion Matsuri|ja|七尾祇園祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanao Gion Matsuri|1=Nanao Gion Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353501}} - Early July * {{ill|Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|ja|向田の火祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|qq=}} - Last Saturday of July * {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|1=Issaki Hōtō Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585601}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}}
; Wajima * Wajima Taisai - August 22–25<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}}
; Suzu * {{ill|Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|ja|飯田町燈籠山祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|1=Iida-machi Toroyama Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666571}} - Late July * {{ill|Hōraku Matsuri|ja|宝立七夕キリコ祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraku Matsuri|1=Hōraku Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452663}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Kiriko Festival Facilities == === Wajima Kiriko Art Museum === {{Main|Wajima Kiriko Art Museum}} The museum in [[:en:Wajima, Ishikawa|Wajima]] showcases 31 kiriko lanterns and explains the festivals.<ref name="asa20220529"/> Opened in 2015, it serves as a cultural hub for the events.<ref>[https://wajima-kiriko.com/ Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]</ref>

== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kiriko Matsuri (asteroid)|ja|キリコ祭り (小惑星)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiriko Matsuri (asteroid)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=91890 Kiriko Matsuri|WD=Q2264843}}

== Kiriko == [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (13).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lanterns on display at the Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]] [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (6).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lantern with auspicious characters]]
Kiriko lanterns are typically tall, rectangular {{ill|Andon|ja|行灯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon|1=andon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626165}} with "auspicious characters" (吉祥文字) representing prayers and wishes inscribed on the front.<ref name="bluesignal"/> The back often features elaborate paintings, such as warrior imagery or landscapes.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} The lanterns are topped with a [[:en:Gable roof|Kirizuma roof]] and adorned with decorations like paper streamers and {{ill|Chōchin|ja|提灯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōchin|1=chōchin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15992049}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} Designs and dimensions vary by region, with some kiriko resembling the curved shapes of [[:en:Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|Nebuta]] floats.

== Major Kiriko Festivals == Dates vary annually. Listed below are notable examples:
; Nanao * {{ill|Mutomachi Festival|ja|互市祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutomachi Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - Early July * {{ill|Nanao Gion Matsuri|ja|七尾祇園祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanao Gion Matsuri|1=Nanao Gion Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353501}} - Early July * {{ill|Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|ja|向田の火祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|qq=}} - Last Saturday of July * {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|1=Issaki Hōtō Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585601}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}}
; Wajima * Wajima Taisai - August 22–25<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}}
; Suzu * {{ill|Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|ja|飯田町燈籠山祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|1=Iida-machi Toroyama Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666571}} - Late July * {{ill|Hōraku Matsuri|ja|宝立七夕キリコ祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraku Matsuri|1=Hōraku Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452663}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Kiriko Festival Facilities == === Wajima Kiriko Art Museum === {{Main|Wajima Kiriko Art Museum}} The museum in [[:en:Wajima, Ishikawa|Wajima]] showcases 31 kiriko lanterns and explains the festivals.<ref name="asa20220529"/> Opened in 2015, it serves as a cultural hub for the events.<ref>[https://wajima-kiriko.com/ Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]</ref>

<!--Noto Kiriko Festivals--><!--Noto Kiriko Festivals--><!--Noto Kiriko Festivals--><!--Noto Kiriko Festivals--><!--Noto Kiriko Festivals--><!--Noto Kiriko Festivals--><!--Noto Kiriko Festivals--><!--Noto Kiriko Festivals--><!--Noto Kiriko Festivals--><!--Noto Kiriko Festivals-->
<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11612004}} [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Festivals in Ishikawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Japan Heritage]] [[Category:Noto Kiriko Matsuri]] [[Category:Noto Kiriko Matsuri|*]] [[Category:Plague warding]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:能登キリコ祭り]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:能登キリコ祭り]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:能登キリコ祭り]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11612004]] Wikidata name: Noto Kiriko Matsuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Redirect|Noto Kiriko Matsuri|asteroid|Kiriko Matsuri (asteroid)}} {{Referencing style|date=December 2022}} [[File:Noto Yosakoi Wakura 01.jpg|thumb|right|300px|The {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|1=Issaki Hōtō Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585601}} held in [[:en:Nanao, Ishikawa|Nanao]].]]
'''Noto Kiriko Matsuri''' (能登キリコ祭り) are festivals held across the [[:en:Noto Peninsula|Noto Peninsula]] in [[:en:Ishikawa Prefecture|Ishikawa Prefecture]].<ref name="bluesignal">{{Cite journal|title=Travel West to Japan Heritage: Noto's Festivals that Invite the Gods|journal=Blue Signal|publisher=[[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]]|date=July 2019|issue=July 2019|url=https://www.westjr.co.jp/company/info/issue/bsignal/19_vol_185/issue/01.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="sankeiphoto">{{Cite web|title=The Sublime Beauty of Japan: Lantern Festivals on the Noto Peninsula|website=Sankei Photo|date=October 2, 2019|url=https://www.sankei.com/photo/story/news/191006/sty1910060001-n1.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="asa20220529">{{Cite news|title=Noto's Iconic Kiriko Festivals: Taō Tsuchiya and the Self-Defense Forces Join In|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun Digital|date=May 29, 2022|url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20220529163550/https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archivedate=May 29, 2022|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref>.
== Overview == The festivals are characterized by the use of tall, rectangular lantern floats called {{ill|Kiriko|ja|切籠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiriko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Hōtō|ja|奉燈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōtō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} While some kiriko lanterns exceed 10 meters in height, most are between 4 to 6 meters and are carried by groups of around 20 people.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} In some areas, they are also referred to as "Oakashii" (御明かし). The festivals typically feature processions through town streets to a temporary shrine (often located near water or in open spaces) before returning to the main shrine.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Some festivals include sea crossings during their processions.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
Held primarily from July to September as summer or autumn festivals, they occur at about 200 locations throughout the peninsula.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="sankeiphoto"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Many of these festivals originated with prayers for the prevention of epidemics<ref name="bluesignal"/> or for bountiful harvests and fishing.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== History == The origins of the Kiriko Matsuri are partly rooted in oral traditions, with limited documentation. One of the earliest references to kiriko can be found in the 1647 (Shōhō 3) festival records of {{ill|Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|輪島住吉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> The {{ill|Abare Matsuri|ja|あばれ祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abare Matsuri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Abare Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4663470}} of {{ill|Ushitsu|ja|宇出津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ushitsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, held at {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社 (能登町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasaka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, is said to have started during the Kanbun era (1661–1673), although this is debated.<ref name="asa20220529"/> The festivals are generally believed to have originated during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and derive either from the [[:en:Gion Matsuri|Gion Matsuri]] in Kyoto or from midsummer Shinto rites.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
In December 1997 (Heisei 9), the "Noto Kiriko Matsuri" was designated as a {{ill|Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|1=Intangible Folk Cultural Property that need measures such as documentation of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642584}} by the national government.<ref>{{Cultural Heritage Online|161401|Noto's Kiriko Festivals}}</ref> In 2015, the festivals were recognized as part of the Japan Heritage program under the title "Islands of Light: Noto Peninsula's Passionate Kiriko Festivals."<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref>[http://www.bunka.go.jp/seisaku/bunkazai/nihon_isan/ichiran.html List of Japan Heritage Sites] - Agency for Cultural Affairs</ref>
== Kiriko == [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (13).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lanterns on display at the Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]] [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (6).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lantern with auspicious characters]]
Kiriko lanterns are typically tall, rectangular {{ill|Andon|ja|行灯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon|1=andon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626165}} with "auspicious characters" (吉祥文字) representing prayers and wishes inscribed on the front.<ref name="bluesignal"/> The back often features elaborate paintings, such as warrior imagery or landscapes.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} The lanterns are topped with a [[:en:Gable roof|Kirizuma roof]] and adorned with decorations like paper streamers and {{ill|Chōchin|ja|提灯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōchin|1=chōchin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15992049}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} Designs and dimensions vary by region, with some kiriko resembling the curved shapes of [[:en:Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|Nebuta]] floats.
It is believed that kiriko originated as small, hand-held bamboo sticks with paper lanterns ("Sasa Kiriko") and gradually grew larger, reaching their current size by the late Edo period.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> However, depopulation has led to a shortage of carriers in some regions, prompting adjustments like rescheduling festivals to weekends.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Major Kiriko Festivals == Dates vary annually. Listed below are notable examples:
; Nanao * {{ill|Mutomachi Festival|ja|互市祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutomachi Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - Early July * {{ill|Nanao Gion Matsuri|ja|七尾祇園祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanao Gion Matsuri|1=Nanao Gion Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353501}} - Early July * {{ill|Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|ja|向田の火祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|qq=}} - Last Saturday of July * {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|1=Issaki Hōtō Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585601}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}}
; Wajima * Wajima Taisai - August 22–25<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}}
; Suzu * {{ill|Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|ja|飯田町燈籠山祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|1=Iida-machi Toroyama Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666571}} - Late July * {{ill|Hōraku Matsuri|ja|宝立七夕キリコ祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraku Matsuri|1=Hōraku Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452663}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Kiriko Festival Facilities == === Wajima Kiriko Art Museum === {{Main|Wajima Kiriko Art Museum}} The museum in [[:en:Wajima, Ishikawa|Wajima]] showcases 31 kiriko lanterns and explains the festivals.<ref name="asa20220529"/> Opened in 2015, it serves as a cultural hub for the events.<ref>[https://wajima-kiriko.com/ Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]</ref>

'''Noto Kiriko Matsuri''' (能登キリコ祭り) are festivals held across the [[:en:Noto Peninsula|Noto Peninsula]] in [[:en:Ishikawa Prefecture|Ishikawa Prefecture]].<ref name="bluesignal">{{Cite journal|title=Travel West to Japan Heritage: Noto's Festivals that Invite the Gods|journal=Blue Signal|publisher=[[:en:West Japan Railway Company|JR West]]|date=July 2019|issue=July 2019|url=https://www.westjr.co.jp/company/info/issue/bsignal/19_vol_185/issue/01.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="sankeiphoto">{{Cite web|title=The Sublime Beauty of Japan: Lantern Festivals on the Noto Peninsula|website=Sankei Photo|date=October 2, 2019|url=https://www.sankei.com/photo/story/news/191006/sty1910060001-n1.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="asa20220529">{{Cite news|title=Noto's Iconic Kiriko Festivals: Taō Tsuchiya and the Self-Defense Forces Join In|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun Digital|date=May 29, 2022|url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20220529163550/https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archivedate=May 29, 2022|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref>.
== Overview == The festivals are characterized by the use of tall, rectangular lantern floats called {{ill|Kiriko|ja|切籠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiriko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Hōtō|ja|奉燈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōtō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} While some kiriko lanterns exceed 10 meters in height, most are between 4 to 6 meters and are carried by groups of around 20 people.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} In some areas, they are also referred to as "Oakashii" (御明かし). The festivals typically feature processions through town streets to a temporary shrine (often located near water or in open spaces) before returning to the main shrine.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Some festivals include sea crossings during their processions.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
Held primarily from July to September as summer or autumn festivals, they occur at about 200 locations throughout the peninsula.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="sankeiphoto"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Many of these festivals originated with prayers for the prevention of epidemics<ref name="bluesignal"/> or for bountiful harvests and fishing.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== History == The origins of the Kiriko Matsuri are partly rooted in oral traditions, with limited documentation. One of the earliest references to kiriko can be found in the 1647 (Shōhō 3) festival records of {{ill|Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|輪島住吉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> The {{ill|Abare Matsuri|ja|あばれ祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abare Matsuri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Abare Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4663470}} of {{ill|Ushitsu|ja|宇出津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ushitsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, held at {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社 (能登町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasaka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, is said to have started during the Kanbun era (1661–1673), although this is debated.<ref name="asa20220529"/> The festivals are generally believed to have originated during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and derive either from the [[:en:Gion Matsuri|Gion Matsuri]] in Kyoto or from midsummer Shinto rites.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
In December 1997 (Heisei 9), the "Noto Kiriko Matsuri" was designated as a {{ill|Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|1=Intangible Folk Cultural Property that need measures such as documentation of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642584}} by the national government.<ref>{{Cultural Heritage Online|161401|Noto's Kiriko Festivals}}</ref> In 2015, the festivals were recognized as part of the Japan Heritage program under the title "Islands of Light: Noto Peninsula's Passionate Kiriko Festivals."<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref>[http://www.bunka.go.jp/seisaku/bunkazai/nihon_isan/ichiran.html List of Japan Heritage Sites] - Agency for Cultural Affairs</ref>
== Kiriko == [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (13).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lanterns on display at the Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]] [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (6).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lantern with auspicious characters]]
Kiriko lanterns are typically tall, rectangular {{ill|Andon|ja|行灯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon|1=andon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626165}} with "auspicious characters" (吉祥文字) representing prayers and wishes inscribed on the front.<ref name="bluesignal"/> The back often features elaborate paintings, such as warrior imagery or landscapes.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} The lanterns are topped with a [[:en:Gable roof|Kirizuma roof]] and adorned with decorations like paper streamers and {{ill|Chōchin|ja|提灯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōchin|1=chōchin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15992049}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} Designs and dimensions vary by region, with some kiriko resembling the curved shapes of [[:en:Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|Nebuta]] floats.
It is believed that kiriko originated as small, hand-held bamboo sticks with paper lanterns ("Sasa Kiriko") and gradually grew larger, reaching their current size by the late Edo period.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> However, depopulation has led to a shortage of carriers in some regions, prompting adjustments like rescheduling festivals to weekends.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Major Kiriko Festivals == Dates vary annually. Listed below are notable examples:
; Nanao * {{ill|Mutomachi Festival|ja|互市祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutomachi Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - Early July * {{ill|Nanao Gion Matsuri|ja|七尾祇園祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanao Gion Matsuri|1=Nanao Gion Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353501}} - Early July * {{ill|Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|ja|向田の火祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|qq=}} - Last Saturday of July * {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|1=Issaki Hōtō Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585601}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}}
; Wajima * Wajima Taisai - August 22–25<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}}
; Suzu * {{ill|Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|ja|飯田町燈籠山祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|1=Iida-machi Toroyama Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666571}} - Late July * {{ill|Hōraku Matsuri|ja|宝立七夕キリコ祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraku Matsuri|1=Hōraku Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452663}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:能登キリコ祭り]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:能登キリコ祭り]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:能登キリコ祭り]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11612004]] Wikidata name: Noto Kiriko Matsuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Redirect|Noto Kiriko Matsuri|asteroid|Kiriko Matsuri (asteroid)}} {{Referencing style|date=December 2022}} [[File:Noto Yosakoi Wakura 01.jpg|thumb|right|300px|The {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|1=Issaki Hōtō Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585601}} held in {{ill|Nanao, Ishikawa|en|Nanao, Ishikawa|WD=Q695892|lt=Nanao}}.]]
'''Noto Kiriko Matsuri''' (能登キリコ祭り) are festivals held across the {{ill|Noto Peninsula|en|Noto Peninsula|WD=Q691151|lt=Noto Peninsula}} in {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|en|Ishikawa Prefecture|WD=Q131281|lt=Ishikawa Prefecture}}.<ref name="bluesignal">{{Cite journal|title=Travel West to Japan Heritage: Noto's Festivals that Invite the Gods|journal=Blue Signal|publisher={{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=JR West}}|date=July 2019|issue=July 2019|url=https://www.westjr.co.jp/company/info/issue/bsignal/19_vol_185/issue/01.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="sankeiphoto">{{Cite web|title=The Sublime Beauty of Japan: Lantern Festivals on the Noto Peninsula|website=Sankei Photo|date=October 2, 2019|url=https://www.sankei.com/photo/story/news/191006/sty1910060001-n1.html|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref><ref name="asa20220529">{{Cite news|title=Noto's Iconic Kiriko Festivals: Taō Tsuchiya and the Self-Defense Forces Join In|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun Digital|date=May 29, 2022|url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20220529163550/https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQ5X747GQ5QPISC001.html|archivedate=May 29, 2022|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref>.
== Overview == The festivals are characterized by the use of tall, rectangular lantern floats called {{ill|Kiriko|ja|切籠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiriko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or {{ill|Hōtō|ja|奉燈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōtō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} While some kiriko lanterns exceed 10 meters in height, most are between 4 to 6 meters and are carried by groups of around 20 people.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} In some areas, they are also referred to as "Oakashii" (御明かし). The festivals typically feature processions through town streets to a temporary shrine (often located near water or in open spaces) before returning to the main shrine.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Some festivals include sea crossings during their processions.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
Held primarily from July to September as summer or autumn festivals, they occur at about 200 locations throughout the peninsula.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="sankeiphoto"/><ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}} Many of these festivals originated with prayers for the prevention of epidemics<ref name="bluesignal"/> or for bountiful harvests and fishing.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== History == The origins of the Kiriko Matsuri are partly rooted in oral traditions, with limited documentation. One of the earliest references to kiriko can be found in the 1647 (Shōhō 3) festival records of {{ill|Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|輪島住吉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wajima Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> The {{ill|Abare Matsuri|ja|あばれ祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abare Matsuri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|1=Abare Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4663470}} of {{ill|Ushitsu|ja|宇出津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ushitsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, held at {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社 (能登町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasaka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, is said to have started during the Kanbun era (1661–1673), although this is debated.<ref name="asa20220529"/> The festivals are generally believed to have originated during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} and derive either from the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|en|Gion Matsuri|WD=Q979873|lt=Gion Matsuri}} in Kyoto or from midsummer Shinto rites.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=134}}
In December 1997 (Heisei 9), the "Noto Kiriko Matsuri" was designated as a {{ill|Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Folk Cultural Property Requiring Measures for Documentation|1=Intangible Folk Cultural Property that need measures such as documentation of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11642584}} by the national government.<ref>{{Cultural Heritage Online|161401|Noto's Kiriko Festivals}}</ref> In 2015, the festivals were recognized as part of the Japan Heritage program under the title "Islands of Light: Noto Peninsula's Passionate Kiriko Festivals."<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref>[http://www.bunka.go.jp/seisaku/bunkazai/nihon_isan/ichiran.html List of Japan Heritage Sites] - Agency for Cultural Affairs</ref>
== Kiriko == [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (13).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lanterns on display at the Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]] [[File:Wajima Kiriko Art Museum ac (6).jpg|thumb|Kiriko lantern with auspicious characters]]
Kiriko lanterns are typically tall, rectangular {{ill|Andon|ja|行灯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon|1=andon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626165}} with "auspicious characters" (吉祥文字) representing prayers and wishes inscribed on the front.<ref name="bluesignal"/> The back often features elaborate paintings, such as warrior imagery or landscapes.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} The lanterns are topped with a {{ill|Gable roof|en|Gable roof|WD=Q149617|lt=Kirizuma roof}} and adorned with decorations like paper streamers and {{ill|Chōchin|ja|提灯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōchin|1=chōchin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15992049}}.<ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=135}} Designs and dimensions vary by region, with some kiriko resembling the curved shapes of {{ill|Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|en|Aomori Nebuta Matsuri|WD=Q862407|lt=Nebuta}} floats.
It is believed that kiriko originated as small, hand-held bamboo sticks with paper lanterns ("Sasa Kiriko") and gradually grew larger, reaching their current size by the late Edo period.<ref name="bluesignal"/><ref name="asa20220529"/> However, depopulation has led to a shortage of carriers in some regions, prompting adjustments like rescheduling festivals to weekends.<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Major Kiriko Festivals == Dates vary annually. Listed below are notable examples:
; Nanao * {{ill|Mutomachi Festival|ja|互市祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutomachi Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - Early July * {{ill|Nanao Gion Matsuri|ja|七尾祇園祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanao Gion Matsuri|1=Nanao Gion Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353501}} - Early July * {{ill|Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda Fire Festival|lt=Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|ja|向田の火祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukōda no Hi Matsuri|qq=}} - Last Saturday of July * {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|ja|石崎奉燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri|1=Issaki Hōtō Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585601}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="asa20220529"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=136}}
; Wajima * Wajima Taisai - August 22–25<ref name="asa20220529"/><ref name="bluesignal"/>{{sfn|JTB|2018|p=137}}
; Suzu * {{ill|Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|ja|飯田町燈籠山祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iida Tōrōyama Matsuri|1=Iida-machi Toroyama Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666571}} - Late July * {{ill|Hōraku Matsuri|ja|宝立七夕キリコ祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōraku Matsuri|1=Hōraku Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452663}} - First Saturday of August<ref name="sankeiphoto"/>
== Kiriko Festival Facilities == === Wajima Kiriko Art Museum === {{Main|Wajima Kiriko Art Museum}} The museum in {{ill|Wajima, Ishikawa|en|Wajima, Ishikawa|WD=Q823896|lt=Wajima}} showcases 31 kiriko lanterns and explains the festivals.<ref name="asa20220529"/> Opened in 2015, it serves as a cultural hub for the events.<ref>[https://wajima-kiriko.com/ Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|title=Nippon Dissected: Kanazawa and Noto Peninsula|publisher=JTB Publishing|date=March 1, 2018|isbn=978-4-533-12465-5|ref={{sfnref|JTB|2018}}}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Kiriko Matsuri (asteroid)|ja|キリコ祭り (小惑星)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiriko Matsuri (asteroid)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=91890 Kiriko Matsuri|WD=Q2264843}}
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Noto Kiriko Matsuri}} * [https://www.hot-ishikawa.jp/feature/kirikomatsuri/top Hot Ishikawa Tourism: Kiriko Matsuri] * [https://japan-heritage.bunka.go.jp/ Islands of Light: Noto's Kiriko Festivals] - Japan Heritage
{{Japan Cultural Properties}}
{{translated page|ja|能登キリコ祭り|version=103277151|comment=Imported full ja history}}
